commit_message: Force collections to be static (#68723)
title: Using a variable in FQCN of the collection called inside the playbook does not find the roles

dialogue.author: alexvinog
dialogue.text:
Using a variable in FQCN of the collection called inside the playbook does not find the roles

<!--- Verify first that your issue is not already reported on GitHub -->
<!--- Also test if the latest release and devel branch are affected too -->
<!--- Complete *all* sections as described, this form is processed automatically -->

##### SUMMARY
<!--- Explain the problem briefly below -->
While trying to include roles from collections in a dynamic manner, I used a variable as name for the collection name (eg. my_namespace.{{ collection_var_name }}.role1 ). By doing so, the role is not found. Replacing the collection_var_name with the collection name correctly i can use the role without issues.

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Bug Report

##### COMPONENT NAME
<!--- Write the short name of the module, plugin, task or feature below, use your best guess if unsure -->
Collections

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
<!--- Paste verbatim output from "ansible --version" between quotes -->
```

ansible 2.9.6
  configured module search path = [u'/Users/alexandruvinogradov/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules']
  ansible python module location = /Users/alexandruvinogradov/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/ansible
  executable location = /usr/local/bin/ansible
  python version = 2.7.16 (default, Jan 27 2020, 04:46:15) [GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 10.0.1 (clang-1001.0.37.14)]

```

##### CONFIGURATION
<!--- Paste verbatim output from "ansible-config dump --only-changed" between quotes -->
```
no modification
```

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
<!--- Provide all relevant information below, e.g. target OS versions, network device firmware, etc. -->
MacOS Mojave 10.14.6


##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
<!--- Describe exactly how to reproduce the problem, using a minimal test-case -->

<!--- Paste example playbooks or commands between quotes below -->
Use the playbook below to try to execute role1 from a pre-created collection. Call the collection by passing the var collection_name to match the collection name. 
ansible-playbook playbook.yml  -e'{"collection_name":"name"}'

```
---
- hosts: all  
  collections:
   - namespace.{{ collection_name }}
  tasks:
    - import_role:
        name: role1
```

<!--- HINT: You can paste gist.github.com links for larger files -->

##### EXPECTED RESULTS
<!--- Describe what you expected to happen when running the steps above -->
The expected behaviour would be that the role from the collection passed as variable gets executed. If i were to replace the variable in the playbook with the actual passed name, the role gets executed correctly.


##### ACTUAL RESULTS
<!--- Describe what actually happened. If possible run with extra verbosity (-vvvv) -->
The role is not recognized as part of the collection.
<!--- Paste verbatim command output between quotes -->
```
ERROR! the role 'role1' was not found

The error appears to be in 'playbook.yml': line 7, column 15, but may
be elsewhere in the file depending on the exact syntax problem.

The offending line appears to be:

    - import_role:
        name: role1
              ^ here

```

dialogue.author: sivel
dialogue.text: We've reviewed this and determined that we are not willing to supporting templating values in `collections:` at this time. The associated PR will add warnings to tell users that the field is not templatable, and that the literal values will be used.

commit_message: When no pool quantity is set, then do not set quantity to 1 (#66807)
title: Redhat_subscription fails when quantity isn't given on a physical server.

dialogue.author: AdamMorris23
dialogue.text:
Redhat_subscription fails when quantity isn't given on a physical server.

<!--- Verify first that your issue is not already reported on GitHub -->
<!--- Also test if the latest release and devel branch are affected too -->
<!--- Complete *all* sections as described, this form is processed automatically -->

##### SUMMARY
<!--- Explain the problem briefly below -->
Using the redhat_subscription module with pool_ids and no quantities on a physical server causes the quantity to be forced to 1.  This results in a warning on stdout from redhat_subscription and a return code of 1.  This causes the action to be marked as FAILED although subscription_manager does the right thing.

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Bug Report

##### COMPONENT NAME
<!--- Write the short name of the module, plugin, task or feature below, use your best guess if unsure -->
redhat_subscription

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
<!--- Paste verbatim output from "ansible --version" between quotes -->
```paste below
ansible 2.8.6
  config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg
  configured module search path = [u'/home/morrisa/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules']
  ansible python module location = /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible
  executable location = /usr/bin/ansible
  python version = 2.7.5 (default, Jun 11 2019, 14:33:56) [GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-39)]
```

##### CONFIGURATION
<!--- Paste verbatim output from "ansible-config dump --only-changed" between quotes -->
```paste below
DEFAULT_ROLES_PATH(/etc/ansible/ansible.cfg) = [u'/etc/ansible/roles', u'/usr/share/ansible/roles']
```

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
<!--- Provide all relevant information below, e.g. target OS versions, network device firmware, etc. -->
Tested with Ansible 2.9, not tested with the development version yet but the issue is still there in the code.  Target OS Version is RedHat Enterprise Linux 7.6 on a two socket physical server.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
<!--- Describe exactly how to reproduce the problem, using a minimal test-case -->
Using a physical server with two sockets execute the equivalent of the yaml below.

<!--- Paste example playbooks or commands between quotes below -->
```yaml
- name: Same as above but subscribe to a specific pool by ID.
  redhat_subscription:
    state: present
    username: joe_user
    password: somepass
    pool_ids: 0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef
```

<!--- HINT: You can paste gist.github.com links for larger files -->

##### EXPECTED RESULTS
<!--- Describe what you expected to happen when running the steps above -->
I expect the system to be subscribed to a given pool.

##### ACTUAL RESULTS
<!--- Describe what actually happened. If possible run with extra verbosity (-vvvv) -->

This play fails with a warning on stdout that Quantity needs to be a multiple of 2 and a return code of 1.  Subscription-manager does the right thing but the return code causes Ansible to choke.

In the main code if pool_ids is provided without a quantity then it is set to 1.  This is not valid for a two socket physical server.

<!--- Paste verbatim command output between quotes -->
```paste below

```

dialogue.author: jirihnidek
dialogue.text:
When quantity is not specified, then default value 1 is used: https://github.com/ansible/ansible/blob/1152774f923e441ec2e71c380cdb78e7f44f94ea/lib/ansible/modules/packaging/os/redhat_subscription.py#L842

This works in most cases, but not in case described in this issue. When special type of product is used, then it is required to attach at least quantity e.g. 2 on physical server. When no quantity is specified, then subscription-manager is able to attach correct minimal quantity as it is demonstrated in following example:

```
[root@localhost ~]# subscription-manager attach --pool ff8080816fd8736f016fd87669c702b5 --quantity=1
Subscription "Awesome OS Instance Based (Standard Support)" must be attached using a quantity evenly divisible by 2
[root@localhost ~]# subscription-manager attach --pool ff8080816fd8736f016fd87669c702b5
Successfully attached a subscription for: Awesome OS Instance Based (Standard Support)
```

When quantity is not specified, then I propose to set quantity to `None` and not set `--quantity` option here: https://github.com/ansible/ansible/blob/1152774f923e441ec2e71c380cdb78e7f44f94ea/lib/ansible/modules/packaging/os/redhat_subscription.py#L518

@cnsnyder Thoughts?

commit_message: Change exit code to 2 when config file doesn't exist (#1361)
title: Black --check returns 1 when invalid config file is specified

dialogue.author: tobywf
dialogue.text:
Black --check returns 1 when invalid config file is specified

**Describe the bug**

`--check` returns `1` when invalid `--config` is specified, not some higher error code as implied. This can make debugging issues tricky for CI integrations relying on return codes. I wouldn't go so far as to call it a bug, but it was a bit unexpected to me.

(It also happens without `--check`.)

**To Reproduce**

```console
$ printf "def a():\n    pass\n" | black - --check; echo "return: $?"
def a():
    pass
All done! ✨ 🍰 ✨
1 file left unchanged.
return: 0
$ printf "def a():\n    pass\n" | black - --check --config "foo"; echo "return: $?"
Error: Could not open file foo: Error reading configuration file: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'foo'
return: 1
```

**Expected behavior**

`--check` says:

> Don't write the files back, just return the status.  Return code 0 means nothing would change.  Return code 1 means some files would be reformatted.  Return code 123 means there was an internal error.

So I would expect this case to return an error code other than `1`.

**Environment (please complete the following information):**

- Version: `19.10b0`, `master` (both tested)
- OS and Python version: macOS 10.15.3 with Python 3.7.7; and Ubuntu 18.04.4 with Python 3.6.9

**Does this bug also happen on master?**

Yes (tested via cloning and `pipenv shell`)

**Additional context**

This can be very awkward to debug in a CI environment if you rely on return codes only.

dialogue.author: tobywf
dialogue.text:
I think this could be quite an easy fix. I'm not sure why, but `--config` doesn't require the file to exist:

https://github.com/psf/black/blob/8654e8d7aef5253faa7f8ff31d4be3b0b8b9c392/black.py#L394-L402

`src` does:

https://github.com/psf/black/blob/8654e8d7aef5253faa7f8ff31d4be3b0b8b9c392/black.py#L386-L393

If `exists=True`, then this becomes a [`BadParameter`](https://github.com/pallets/click/blob/97de2c97275f52adc5c6ef8bb8aa993ce997fe7e/src/click/exceptions.py#L66) click exceptions, which inherits from [`UsageError`](https://github.com/pallets/click/blob/97de2c97275f52adc5c6ef8bb8aa993ce997fe7e/src/click/exceptions.py#L34), which has [`exit_code = 2`](https://github.com/pallets/click/blob/97de2c97275f52adc5c6ef8bb8aa993ce997fe7e/src/click/exceptions.py#L43), as does any other invalid parameter.

Instead, it progresses until it tries to read the config file:

https://github.com/psf/black/blob/8654e8d7aef5253faa7f8ff31d4be3b0b8b9c392/black.py#L251-L256

This raises a [`FileError`](https://github.com/pallets/click/blob/97de2c97275f52adc5c6ef8bb8aa993ce997fe7e/src/click/exceptions.py#L204), which inherits from [`ClickException`](https://github.com/pallets/click/blob/97de2c97275f52adc5c6ef8bb8aa993ce997fe7e/src/click/exceptions.py#L12), which has [`exit_code = 1`](https://github.com/pallets/click/blob/97de2c97275f52adc5c6ef8bb8aa993ce997fe7e/src/click/exceptions.py#L16)

With `exists=True`, this happens instead:

```console
$ printf "def a():\n    pass\n" | black --check --config "foo" -; echo "return: $?"
Usage: black [OPTIONS] [SRC]...
Try 'black -h' for help.

Error: Invalid value for '--config': File 'foo' does not exist.
return: 2
```

The case where no file is specified still works, too.

Happy to create a pull request if this sounds like a good solution to you?

dialogue.author: JelleZijlstra
dialogue.text: Switching to `exists=True` sounds like the right call. Please send a PR!

commit_message: Fix trailing comma for function with one arg (#880) (#891)
title: Trailing comma for function with one arg

dialogue.author: cosinequanon
dialogue.text:
Trailing comma for function with one arg

A function with one argument keeps the trailing comma but if there are two it is stripped.

In:
```
def one(
    a,
):
    pass

def two(
    a,
    b,
):
    pass
```
Out
```
def one(a,):
    pass


def two(a, b):
    pass
```
From https://black.now.sh/?version=stable&state=_Td6WFoAAATm1rRGAgAhARYAAAB0L-Wj4ACGAFNdAD2IimZxl1N_WlbvK5V-4TOOTwYyJEleH-JCdis69M0BXQy1O1PWCxUpRscYAufHo8BjrjIj1dM6YL_cyWPemFXxt1cpBhl0f8lWpI3sZ5u99cYAAACFuqAGuJSBnwABb4cBAAAAtWKNgbHEZ_sCAAAAAARZWg==

dialogue.author: zsol
dialogue.text:
That's a bug, `one` should be formatted like so:
```
def one(a):
    pass
```

dialogue.author: djb7
dialogue.text: I made pull request #891 with a potential fix. Please let me know if the approach makes sense and if you have any suggestions for improvement.

dialogue.author: Jma353
dialogue.text: Looks like this was fixed in 9394de150ebf0adc426523f46dc08e8b2b2b0b63.  Can this be closed?

dialogue.author: zsol
dialogue.text: yep.

commit_message: Add support for always tokenizing async/await as keywords
title: Black can't parse new Python 3.7 async generator syntax

dialogue.author: DRMacIver
dialogue.text:
Black can't parse new Python 3.7 async generator syntax

Operating system: Linux but unlikely to be OS specific
Python version: 3.7.1
*Black* version: 18.9b0
Does also happen on master: Yes (as of  32eed7d)

The following code (extracted from [test_asyncgen.py](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/test/test_asyncgen.py) in CPython) is currently not handled by black:

```python
def make_arange(n):
    # This syntax is legal starting with Python 3.7
    return (i * 2 for i in range(n) if await wrap(i))
```

Running black on it results in:

```
error: cannot format test_asyncgen.py: Cannot parse: 3:45:     return (i * 2 for i in range(n) if await wrap(i))
All done! 💥 💔 💥
1 file failed to reformat.
```

As per the comment, this is new Python 3.7 syntax, so presumably "just" a case of needing to update the parser to reflect the grammar change.

here's another variant, inside the "for" for a list comprehension:

```python
def foo():

        (None, state_dict, params, mapper,
```

commit_message: Fix print() function on Python 2 (#754)
title: black inserts unnecessary whitespace around print() with target_version = py27

dialogue.author: rouge8
dialogue.text:
black inserts unnecessary whitespace around print() with target_version = py27

black is adding whitespace between `print` and `(` with `--target-version py27`.

**Test file:**

```python
from __future__ import print_function

print("hello world")
```

**Result:**

```console
$ black t.py --target-version py27 --diff
--- t.py	2019-03-14 22:09:34.902887 +0000
+++ t.py	2019-03-14 22:10:21.022372 +0000
@@ -1,4 +1,4 @@
 from __future__ import print_function

-print("hello world")
+print ("hello world")

reformatted t.py
All done! ✨ 🍰 ✨
1 file reformatted.
```

Operating system: OS X 
Python version: 3.7.2
*Black* version: 19.3b0
Does also happen on master: yes

commit_message: Fix indent calculation with tabs when computing prefixes (#595)
title: Indentation is incorrectly changed for tabulated comments after a dedent

dialogue.author: sciyoshi
dialogue.text:
Indentation is incorrectly changed for tabulated comments after a dedent

Operating system: Ubuntu 18.04
Python version: 3.6.3
Black version: master

Thank you @ambv for this library. When trying to run this on a codebase that uses tabs, indentation for comments past depth 0 is changed incorrectly after a dedent. Sample input (NB tabs):

```
if 1:
	if 2:
		pass
	# This comment should be indented the same as the next line
	pass
```

Black reformats this to:

```
if 1:
    if 2:
        pass
        # This comment should be indented the same as the next line
    pass
```

Note that this only happens when the input file uses tabs.

dialogue.author: ashwinvis
dialogue.text:
Probably related. Here is another example:

```python
In [1]: import black

In [2]: black.__version__
Out[2]: '18.6b2'

In [3]: cat test_if.py
if True:
    print("A1")
    # print("A2")
else:
    print("B1")
    # print("B2")


In [4]: cat test_if_black.py
if True:
    print("A1")
# print("A2")
else:
    print("B1")
# print("B2")
```

`test_if.py` is the original file and `test_if_black.py` is the reformatted version.

dialogue.author: sciyoshi
dialogue.text:
@ambv I've opened a PR (#595) that fixes this issue and includes a minimal reproduction as a test case. Thanks again for this project!

As an aside, I would implore you to please re-consider adding support for tabs - this feature is the one thing that is blocking our team from using Black. (In the meantime, we will be using [@jleclanche's fork](https://github.com/jleclanche/tan).)

In fact I can't repro this with `18.4a0` either.

dialogue.author: ambv
dialogue.text:
Your example should be a synchronous def:
```py3
def make_arange(n):
    # This syntax is legal starting with Python 3.7
    return (i * 2 async for i in arange(n))
```

(note: not an `async def`)

This is perfectly valid in 3.7+ as you are only *producing* an async gen. You are not consuming it.

dialogue.author: virtuald
dialogue.text:
fmt: off is ignored after first function

Fedora 28, master black

Trying to work around #334 and ran into this.

Minimized test source:

```
import pytest

TmSt = 1
TmEx = 2

# fmt: off

# Test data:
#   Position, Volume, State, TmSt/TmEx/None, [call, [arg1...]]

@pytest.mark.parametrize('test', [

    # Test don't manage the volume
    [
        ('stuff', 'in')
    ],
])
def test_fader(test):
    pass

def check_fader(test):
    pass

def test_calculate_fades():
    calcs = [
        # one is zero/none
        (0, 4, 0, 0, 10,        0, 0, 6, 10),
        (None, 4, 0, 0, 10,     0, 0, 6, 10),
    ]

# fmt: on
```

Result:

```
import pytest

TmSt = 1
TmEx = 2

# fmt: off

# Test data:
#   Position, Volume, State, TmSt/TmEx/None, [call, [arg1...]]

@pytest.mark.parametrize('test', [

    # Test don't manage the volume
    [
        ('stuff', 'in')
    ],
])
def test_fader(test):
    pass


def check_fader(test):
    pass


def test_calculate_fades():
    calcs = [
        # one is zero/none
        (0, 4, 0, 0, 10, 0, 0, 6, 10),
        (None, 4, 0, 0, 10, 0, 0, 6, 10),
    ]


# fmt: on

```

Note that there are two things ignored:

* Inserted extra lines between functions
* the list of tuples in test_calculate_fades was reformatted

dialogue.author: ambv
dialogue.text: Thanks for your repro! Will get to those pesky `# fmt: off` issues tomorrow.

commit_message: Ignore symbolic links pointing outside of the root directory (#339)
title: Crash if there is a symlink to a resource outside of the root directory

dialogue.author: Neraste
dialogue.text:
Crash if there is a symlink to a resource outside of the root directory

Hello there, thank you for this great tool!

Black crashes when it finds a symbolic link pointing to a file or a directory outside of the root directory.

**Steps to reproduce**

- Create the following structure:
```
base
  |-- repo
  |     |-- dummy.py
  |     `-- resource -> ../resource
  `-- resource
```
- Initiate `base/repo` as a Git repository (so as to set the root directory);
- Call `black .` within `base/repo`;
- Expected behavior: *Black* ends as expected;
- Current behavior: throws an exception:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/neraste/.virtualenvs/test_black/bin/black", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/home/neraste/.virtualenvs/test_black/lib/python3.6/site-packages/click/core.py", line 722, in __call__
    return self.main(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/neraste/.virtualenvs/test_black/lib/python3.6/site-packages/click/core.py", line 697, in main
    rv = self.invoke(ctx)
  File "/home/neraste/.virtualenvs/test_black/lib/python3.6/site-packages/click/core.py", line 895, in invoke
    return ctx.invoke(self.callback, **ctx.params)
  File "/home/neraste/.virtualenvs/test_black/lib/python3.6/site-packages/click/core.py", line 535, in invoke
    return callback(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/neraste/.virtualenvs/test_black/lib/python3.6/site-packages/click/decorators.py", line 17, in new_func
    return f(get_current_context(), *args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/neraste/.virtualenvs/test_black/lib/python3.6/site-packages/black.py", line 347, in main
    gen_python_files_in_dir(p, root, include_regex, exclude_regex, report)
  File "/home/neraste/.virtualenvs/test_black/lib/python3.6/site-packages/black.py", line 2942, in gen_python_files_in_dir
    normalized_path = "/" + child.resolve().relative_to(root).as_posix()
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/pathlib.py", line 872, in relative_to
    .format(str(self), str(formatted)))
ValueError: '/data/test_black/base/resource' does not start with '/data/test_black/base/repo'
```

As pointed out in the backtrace, this is due to the line 2942, where the resolved path of the files is made relative to the root directory. This is indeed impossible if the file is outside of the root directory. One can wonder if it makes sense to have an external link within a repository, but, well I am in this case.

Adding the conflicting file/folder to the exclude list does not work.

**Possible workaround**

A possible workaround could be to simply ignore this case and do not handle this simlinked resource:
```python
        try:
            normalized_path = "/" + child.resolve().relative_to(root).as_posix()

        except ValueError:
            if child.is_symlink():
                continue                                                        

            raise
```


**Additional information**

Operating system: GNU/Linux OpenSUSE Tumbleweed
Python version: 3.6
*Black* version: 18.6b2
Does also happen on master: yes

dialogue.author: ambv
dialogue.text:
@Neraste, opportunity for impact! I will accept your pull request to do this. The only small trick is a test case for this :-)

Thanks for the report!

dialogue.author: Neraste
dialogue.text: OK, I will do that.

dialogue.author: alvarocavalcanti
dialogue.text:
Just to add to this point:

> One can wonder if it makes sense to have an external link within a repository, but, well I am in this case.

If you have a `virtual environment` folder as part of your code base, you will see this error, as the folder will have a symlink for the Python interpreter as follows:

```bash
lrwxr-xr-x   1 alvarocavalcanti  staff   79  1 Fev 16:05 .Python -> /usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.6.3/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/Python
```

commit_message: [#154] Handle comments between decorators properly (#166)
title: Adds blank lines after comment between decorators

dialogue.author: blueyed
dialogue.text:
Adds blank lines after comment between decorators

Given t.py:
```python
@property
# TODO: X
@property
def foo():
    pass
```

Running `black --diff t.py` gives:
```diff
--- t-black.py  (original)
+++ t-black.py  (formatted)
@@ -1,6 +1,8 @@
 @property
 # TODO: X
+
+
 @property
 def foo():
     pass
 ```

Where flake8 then complains:

> stdin:5:1: E304 blank lines found after function decorator

Operating system: Arch Linux
Python version: 3.6.5
Black version: 18.4a2
Does also happen on master: Haven't checked.

commit_message: Show full path on diffs
title: --diff doesn't display full file path

dialogue.author: keysmashes
dialogue.text:
--diff doesn't display full file path

Operating system: Ubuntu 16.04.4
Python version: 3.6.3
Black version: 18.4a2
Does also happen on master: I believe so

Doing `black --diff path/to/foo.py` results in:

```diff
--- foo.py  (original)
+++ foo.py  (formatted)
@@ -39,24 +39,21 @@
...
```

caused by the following two lines:

https://github.com/ambv/black/blob/f294cc272c3aa5b8d3cd35b9f9283721b7e458d8/black.py#L298-L299

IMO, Black should print the full path to each file it formats in `--diff` output to be consistent with the behaviour of other diff-producing programs. Not printing the full path also makes it hard to read several concatenated diffs (especially when filenames are duplicated in different directories).

dialogue.author: ambv
dialogue.text: Agreed. I didn't think this through when I was implementing that. A full path is clearly important for validity of the diff if multiple paths were given.

dialogue.author: ambv
dialogue.text: Is it fine that we're using the `(original)` and `(formatted)` conventions? Git is using `a/` and `b/` prepended to the path but I find this less readable.

dialogue.author: keysmashes
dialogue.text: I think the `(original)`/`(formatted)` will make the diff impossible to apply; I'm not sure if that really matters though, since if you have a diff then you could presumably just run Black on the code.

dialogue.author: ambv
dialogue.text: Yeah, I guess if I put "original" and "formatted" *before* the path then the user can use `patch -p1`.

dialogue.author: wbolster
dialogue.text:
Another example of this with long SQLAlchemy queries containing some inline comments in long chained calls like `x.filter().order_by().all()`:

```python
def foo(list_a, list_b):
    results = (
        User.query
        .filter(User.foo == 'bar')  # Because foo.
        .filter(db.or_(
            User.field_a.astext.in_(list_a),
            User.field_b.astext.in_(list_b),
        ))
        .filter(User.xyz.is_(None))
        # Another comment about the filtering on is_quux goes here.
        .filter(db.not_(
            User.is_pending.astext.cast(db.Boolean).is_(True)))
        .order_by(User.created_at.desc())
        .with_for_update(key_share=True)
        .all())
    return results
```

becomes

``` python
def foo(list_a, list_b):
    results = (
        User.query.filter(User.foo == 'bar').filter(db.or_(User.field_a.astext.in_(list_a), User.field_b.astext.in_(list_b))).filter(User.xyz.is_(None)).filter(db.not_(User.is_pending.astext.cast(db.Boolean).is_(True))).order_by(User.created_at.desc()).with_for_update(key_share=True).all()  # Because foo.
        # Another comment about the filtering on is_quux goes here.
    )
    return results
```

This produces extremely long lines, and moves the comments around to the wrong places.

dialogue.author: ambv
dstufft
dialogue.text: Damn, I find the fluent interfaces way nicer for SQLAlchemy queries :) ah well.

```patch python
+        return exec ("code", {}, {})
````

Python version: 3.6.4

commit_message: :bug: use media_type from Body params for OpenAPI requestBody (Fixes: #431) (#439)
title: OpenAPI: media_types of Body parameters and requestBody do not match

dialogue.author: divums
dialogue.text:
OpenAPI: media_types of Body parameters and requestBody do not match

**Describe the bug**
The openapi.json for requestBody definitions uses the FastAPI default media_type instead of the defined one in the Body parameter(s).

**To Reproduce**
Create a path with request body that has either a single embedded parameter or multiple ones. List media_type that is different than the default application/json is. 

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from starlette import status
from . import schemas

app = FastAPI()

@app.post('/customers',
         status_code=status.HTTP_201_CREATED,
         )
def customer_create(data: schemas.Customer = Body(..., media_type='application/vnd+json.api', embed=True)):
    created_customer = {**data.dict(), **{'id': '1'}}
    return {'data': created_customer}
```

Then verify your openapi.json.

```yaml
# ...
post:
  responses:
    # ...
  summary: Customer Create
  operationId: customer_create_api_customers_post
  requestBody:
    content:
      application/json: # this should be different
        schema:
          "$ref": "#/components/schemas/Body_customer_create_api_customers_post"
    required: true
```

**Expected behavior**
The media_type of the requestBody of given path matches the specified one:

```yaml
# ...
post:
  responses:
    # ...
  summary: Customer Create
  operationId: customer_create_api_customers_post
  requestBody:
    content:
      application/vnd+json.api:
        schema:
          "$ref": "#/components/schemas/Body_customer_create_api_customers_post"
    required: true
```

**Environment:**
 - OS: masOS 10.14.6
 - Python: 3.6.5
 - FastAPI: 0.35.0

commit_message: Fixed custom Host
title: Duplicate Host headers when custom Host specified

dialogue.author: perbu
dialogue.text:
Duplicate Host headers when custom Host specified

Version: 0.8.0-1 (ubuntu).

When overriding the HTTP Host header you'll end up with duplicate headers if the casing of the command line option isn't correct. 

``` bash
$ http -pHh http://localhost/cgi-bin/test.cgi host:www.foo.com
GET /cgi-bin/test.cgi HTTP/1.1
Accept: */*
Host: localhost
host: www.foo.com
```

**If I specify `Host:www.foo.com` all is well.** `User-Agent` seems to be handled correctly, so I guess there is some thought given to it.

PS: httpie is a great tool. I like it a lot.

dialogue.author: jkbrzt
dialogue.text: Thanks for the report. There should be a case-insensitive check somewhere instead.

dialogue.author: perbu
dialogue.text:
Yeah. If I recall correctly all HTTP headers are case insensitive so the
logic should be global, not just limited to user-agent.

commit_message: Fix parsing for TupleParameter (#2619)
title: TupleParameter errors in dynamic dependencies

dialogue.author: duckontheweb
dialogue.text:
TupleParameter errors in dynamic dependencies

Passing a `TupleParameter` in a dynamic dependency results in a `TypeError` in Python 3.6.5. See the following example for a basic demonstration.

The following code:

```python
import luigi
import luigi.mock
import luigi.util

class FirstTask(luigi.Task):

    # my_tuple = luigi.ListParameter(default=(1, 2, 3))
    my_tuple = luigi.TupleParameter(default=(1, 2, 3))

    @property
    def tuple_string(self):
        return '-'.join(map(str, self.my_tuple))

    def run(self):
        print(self.task_id + ': ' + self.tuple_string)
        with self.output().open('w') as f:
            f.write(self.tuple_string)

    def output(self):
        return luigi.mock.MockTarget(self.task_id)


@luigi.util.inherits(FirstTask)
class SecondTask(luigi.Task):

    def run(self):
        input_target = yield FirstTask(my_tuple=tuple(self.my_tuple))

        with input_target.open('r') as src:
            my_tuple = src.read()
            print(self.task_id + ': ' + my_tuple)

            with self.output().open('w') as dst:
                dst.write(my_tuple)

    def output(self):
        return luigi.mock.MockTarget(self.task_id)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    luigi.build([SecondTask()], local_scheduler=True)
```

... results in this error:

```console
$ python scratch_9.py
DEBUG: Checking if SecondTask(my_tuple=[1, 2, 3]) is complete
INFO: Informed scheduler that task   SecondTask__1__2__3__a7a294ba13   has status   PENDING
INFO: Done scheduling tasks
INFO: Running Worker with 1 processes
DEBUG: Asking scheduler for work...
DEBUG: Pending tasks: 1
INFO: [pid 10635] Worker Worker(salt=723375901, workers=1, host=DGJonathanDuckworth.local, username=jduckworth, pid=10635) running   SecondTask(my_tuple=[1, 2, 3])
INFO: [pid 10635] Worker Worker(salt=723375901, workers=1, host=DGJonathanDuckworth.local, username=jduckworth, pid=10635) new requirements      SecondTask(my_tuple=[1, 2, 3])
DEBUG: 1 running tasks, waiting for next task to finish
INFO: Worker Worker(salt=723375901, workers=1, host=DGJonathanDuckworth.local, username=jduckworth, pid=10635) was stopped. Shutting down Keep-Alive thread
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/jduckworth/Library/Preferences/PyCharmCE2018.3/scratches/scratch_9.py", line 40, in <module>
    luigi.build([SecondTask()], local_scheduler=True)
  File "/Users/jduckworth/.virtualenvs/viper-dev/lib/python3.6/site-packages/luigi/interface.py", line 274, in build
    return _schedule_and_run(tasks, worker_scheduler_factory, override_defaults=env_params)['success']
  File "/Users/jduckworth/.virtualenvs/viper-dev/lib/python3.6/site-packages/luigi/interface.py", line 207, in _schedule_and_run
    success &= worker.run()
  File "/Users/jduckworth/.virtualenvs/viper-dev/lib/python3.6/site-packages/luigi/worker.py", line 1162, in run
    self._handle_next_task()
  File "/Users/jduckworth/.virtualenvs/viper-dev/lib/python3.6/site-packages/luigi/worker.py", line 1066, in _handle_next_task
    for module, name, params in new_requirements]
  File "/Users/jduckworth/.virtualenvs/viper-dev/lib/python3.6/site-packages/luigi/worker.py", line 1066, in <listcomp>
    for module, name, params in new_requirements]
  File "/Users/jduckworth/.virtualenvs/viper-dev/lib/python3.6/site-packages/luigi/task_register.py", line 253, in load_task
    return task_cls.from_str_params(params_str)
  File "/Users/jduckworth/.virtualenvs/viper-dev/lib/python3.6/site-packages/luigi/task.py", line 482, in from_str_params
    kwargs[param_name] = param.parse(param_str)
  File "/Users/jduckworth/.virtualenvs/viper-dev/lib/python3.6/site-packages/luigi/parameter.py", line 1107, in parse
    return tuple(tuple(x) for x in json.loads(x, object_pairs_hook=_FrozenOrderedDict))
  File "/Users/jduckworth/.virtualenvs/viper-dev/lib/python3.6/site-packages/luigi/parameter.py", line 1107, in <genexpr>
    return tuple(tuple(x) for x in json.loads(x, object_pairs_hook=_FrozenOrderedDict))
TypeError: 'int' object is not iterable
```

It looks like the problem is that the code [here](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/parameter.py#L1117) catches a `ValueError`, but not a `TypeError`. I haven't tested in Python 2, but I'm assuming a `ValueError` gets thrown in that case and this is just a result of differences in Python versions.

I'm happy to put in a PR if it's helpful.

commit_message: Allow NoneType columns in Redshift COPY job (#2303)
title: Redshift COPY fails in luigi 2.7.1 when columns are not provided

dialogue.author: dmohns
dialogue.text:
Redshift COPY fails in luigi 2.7.1 when columns are not provided

Running Redshift COPY jobs with `columns = None` to prohibit table creation fails in luigi 2.7.1 with 
```
TypeError: object of type 'NoneType' has no len()
```

The root cause seems to be https://github.com/spotify/luigi/pull/2245/files#diff-778ea3db4cccaf4de6564889c5eb670fR338

A possible solution would be to change the line to
```python
if self.columns and len(self.columns) > 0:
```
unless I am missing some reason to explicitly ask only for `len(self.columns)`.


<!---
We use GitHub issues mainly for tracking bugs and feature requests.
Questions for how to use luigi can be sent to the mailing list.

Currently, there are no strict procedures or guidelines for submitting issues.
In short, please just use common sense.

Common sense includes this at bare-minimum:

 * search for similar issues posted before creating a new issue.
 * Use markdown to format all code/logs. Issues which are hard to read
   when rendered on GitHub might be closed with a friendly reminder of this.
 * If applicable, reading relevant parts of the documentation.

Also, add steps to reproduce the bug, if applicable. Sample code would be nice too :)

For more information on how to submit valuable contributions,
see https://opensource.guide/how-to-contribute/#how-to-submit-a-contribution
-->

dialogue.author: dlstadther
dialogue.text:
This seems like a reasonable solution. The motivation here was to handle the [default `column`](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/contrib/rdbms.py#L71) definition or some structure with length > 0. Really, we should ensure that it is not `None` and an iterable with length greater than zero.

Feel free to PR!

commit_message: Fix MRO on tasks using util.requires (#2307)
title: util.inherits and requires use inheritence, breaking the MRO

dialogue.author: sbliven
dialogue.text:
util.inherits and requires use inheritence, breaking the MRO

As discussed in on [the mailing list](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/luigi-user/7m0uZkyyYow), using @inherits messes up the method resolution order and breaks `super()`

```
>>> class Foo(luigi.Task): pass
...
>>> @inherits(Foo)
... class Bar(luigi.Task): pass
...
>>> Bar.__mro__
(<class '__main__.Bar'>, <class '__main__.Bar'>, <class 'luigi.task.Task'>, <type 'object'>)
```

Since Bar is now twice on the list, any calls to super will result in infinite loops.

This problem comes from @inherits using inheritance to modify the class. We should look into whether the class can be monkey-patched instead of subclassed, so that inheritance works as expected.

dialogue.author: demmerichs
dialogue.text:
Just ran into this issue. Using super() in python 3 works though, but I wanted to use the python 2 super(class, self) which results in the infinite loops (for compatibility reasons).
In the task i need to cache a input() since the loading of the file format takes forever. Wanted to do this in the __init__ of the task, and here I need the super() call for the arguments. Is this gonna be fixed soon (or a fix even possible), or a workaround for my (pretty generic) case? Something like a init() function of the base class luigi.Task which I could overwrite perhaps?

dialogue.author: Tarrasch
dialogue.text: @DavidS3141, I don't think anyone is currently working on this. Could you take a try yourself?

dialogue.author: demmerichs
dialogue.text: @Tarrasch this is my try ;) but i am a total newbie in pull requests actually ;) Hope this looks fine to you!

commit_message: retcodes: Understand succesful retries (#1951)
title: The retcode configuration does not consider task retry

dialogue.author: bwtakacy
dialogue.text:
The retcode configuration does not consider task retry

Hi.

 I use the retcode configurations as below in luigi.cfg,
```
[retcode]
already_running=10
missing_data=20
not_run=25
task_failed=30
scheduling_error=35
unhandled_exception=40
```

When an one task was once failed and then succeeded in next retry in scheduling during a whole tasks execution, I expected the return code to be zero but actually was non zero.

For example,  running `examples/foo.py`  with some code change that `Bar(num=7)`  fails at the first time run but succeeds in the second time run,  all the tasks succeeded in the end but the return code is non zero.

```
===== Luigi Execution Summary =====

Scheduled 12 tasks of which:
* 11 ran successfully:
    - 10 examples.Bar(num=0...9)
    - 1 examples.Foo()
* 1 failed:
    - 1 examples.Bar(num=7)

This progress looks :( because there were failed tasks

===== Luigi Execution Summary =====

The program exited via sys.exit(). Exit status: 30
```

It seems that the result of `Bar(num=7)` was doubly counted both in success group and failed group.

dialogue.author: Tarrasch
dialogue.text: Definitely a bug I would say. Failures are normal and if we even know it got resolved we should not alarm anyone. Do you want to take a stab at fixing this? :)

dialogue.author: bwtakacy
dialogue.text:
Thanks for your comments!
I will try to create PR.

commit_message: Caches get_autoconfig_client on a per-thread basis
title: HdfsTarget commands fail when many targets are instantiated

dialogue.author: daveFNbuck
dialogue.text:
HdfsTarget commands fail when many targets are instantiated

I've recently added an existence check to a large mapreduce task for which some input files may be missing. With a large enough set of inputs, it will fail every time. I've simplified it to the following code:

``` python
from luigi.contrib.hdfs import HdfsTarget

many_targets = [HdfsTarget('/') for _ in range(2000)]
all(target.exists() for target in many_targets)
```

This will break if I use any past 1000 or so. Here the client uses snakebite. For a more direct triggering, we can also do

``` python
from snakebite.client import AutoConfigClient

clients = [AutoConfigClient() for _ in range(10000)]
all(client.test('/', exists=True) for client in clients)
```

In either case, the bug goes away if I use a generator expression rather than a list comprehension. The problem is that when I'm dealing with objects coming out of luigi calls like input_hadoop, it's too late for me to decide between lists and iterators. I can code around this by instantiating all of my HdfsTargets with the same client, but I'm not sure this is safe. It could also be fixed in luigi if we had get_autoconfig_client return the same object each time. Is there any reason this wouldn't work?

dialogue.author: Tarrasch
dialogue.text:
Thanks for the report. @daveFNbuck, do you understand what causes this? I don't understand how you got to the conclusion that making `get_autoconfig_client` returning the same objects resolves the issue.

Anyhow I'm pretty sure `get_autoconfig_client` returns different objects each time out of pure convenience of the implementers and that the object is so cheap and there's no need to cache it. Just keep thread/process-safety in mind when refactoring. :)

dialogue.author: daveFNbuck
dialogue.text:
The bug comes from a system limit of how many file streams can be opened. I tried to dig into the snakebite code to figure out exactly where it happens. I think it's in the thrift communication. The problem is that each client opens a new communication pipe, so if too many are used at the same time you'll hit the limit. If every HdfsTarget has the same client, they don't open multiple pipes. The following code does not trigger the bug:

``` python
from luigi.contrib.hdfs import HdfsTarget, get_autoconfig_client

fs = get_autoconfig_client()
many_targets = [HdfsTarget('/', fs=fs) for _ in range(2000)]
all(target.exists() for target in many_targets)
```

The bug also does not get triggered if there's a chance to garbage collect between exist calls, which usually happens during normal luigi operation. It's only when you hold more HdfsTargets than your system limit on open files in memory at the same time and operate on them all that there's a problem.

dialogue.author: Tarrasch
dialogue.text: I see clearly now. Thanks for investigating this. Naturally, I would happily review and accept a PR fixing this :)

dialogue.author: daveFNbuck
dialogue.text: Just wanted to check if there was a good reason not to cache the autoconfig client, maybe threading/multiprocessing issues?

dialogue.author: Tarrasch
dialogue.text: I see, I unfortunately don't know.

dialogue.author: daveFNbuck
dialogue.text: I just ran some tests. It seems to be ok to persist the same client object across processes but not threads. Maybe we can make it safe by checking against threading.current_thread() to make sure we create a new one for each thread?

dialogue.author: Tarrasch
dialogue.text:
I suppose the reason it works across processes is because you don't share the variables or anything like that in Python, hence it actually creates multiple objects. I might be wrong though. ^^

Yea I think your solution sounds sane.

commit_message: Fix #1023 add pruning on get_work of local scheduler
title: Local scheduler does not prune automatically

dialogue.author: soxofaan
dialogue.text:
Local scheduler does not prune automatically

Take these script: a main task that depends on a task that only completes successfully after some time has passed since launch

``` python
import time
import luigi

class DepThatNeedsSomeTime(luigi.Task):
    ready_time = luigi.FloatParameter()

    def run(self):
        assert self.complete()

    def complete(self):
        return  self.ready_time <= time.time()

class MainTask(luigi.WrapperTask):
    launch_time = luigi.FloatParameter(default=time.time())

    def requires(self):
        return [DepThatNeedsSomeTime(self.launch_time + 10)]

if __name__ == '__main__':
    luigi.run()
```

run it with these configs to simplify testing/debugging:

```
python script.py MainTask --scheduler-retry-delay 1 --worker-keep-alive
```

If you run this with central scheduler, the dependency will fail initially, but after some it will be retried because the server calls `scheduler.prune` every minute:

``` python
    # prune work DAG every 60 seconds
    pruner = tornado.ioloop.PeriodicCallback(scheduler.prune, 60000)
```

and `scheduler.prune` causes rescheduling/retrying of the failed task (through triggering `SimpleTaskState.prune`)

So eventually all work will be done successfully.

However if you run this with the local scheduler (additional `--local-scheduler`), you end up in an endless loop because `scheduler.prune` is never called and the failed task never gets a chance on a retry.

(FYI: I lost quite some time figuring all this out when I was examining the retry behaviour of Luigi with the local scheduler, assuming it would work the same as the central scheduler)

commit_message: Fix #896: make table_exists case insensitive
title: hive table_exists should be case insensitive?

dialogue.author: nzlinus
dialogue.text:
hive table_exists should be case insensitive?

Any thoughts on this one?

In https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/contrib/hive.py#L141
(possibly here too, but we're not on CDH: https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/contrib/hive.py#L192)

Sometimes we have tables that are defined as capitalized, rather than lower case underscored names.  These are easier to read in code if left capitalized, though hive is case insensitive, and will return them as lower case.

E.g. when checking for an existing table `table = 'FooBar'`, stdout will return with `foobar` and the test will fail

This wasn't an issue in older versions, which just checked for string "does not exist" or "Table not found" in stdout.

Would be easy to fix using `return stdout and table.lower() in stdout` or `return stdout and table.lower() in stdout.lower()`

Let me know your thoughts on this.  I can supply a pull request if necessary.

Thanks,
Lin.

dialogue.author: Tarrasch
dialogue.text: Please do give a pull request, with an accompanying test if possible :)

commit_message: Positional params: revert behavior to a78338c^
title: Latest Luigi version breaks my hadoop jobs

dialogue.author: daveFNbuck
dialogue.text:
Latest Luigi version breaks my hadoop jobs

I used to be able to initialize a class like

```
class MyStreamingJob(luigi.hadoop.JobTask):
    param = luigi.Parameter()
```

with code like

```
MyStreamingJob('param_value')
```

but now this results in an error

```
luigi.parameter.MissingParameterException: MyStreamingJob[args=('param_value',), kwargs={}]: requires the 'param' parameter to be set
```

I believe it's now interpreting the first argument as pool, where it never did that before.

dialogue.author: erikbern
dialogue.text: Thanks – this seems to be the case. I don't know why our existing tests aren't catching it. Let me try to add a test + fix

dialogue.author: Tarrasch
dialogue.text: You still have broken backwards compatibility when you removed `if not p.is_global`. We had issues when the condition is only `if p.significant` (we had a insignificant global parameter in a Task). I thought to change it to `if p.significant and not p.is_global`. I'll send a PR with tests tomorrow. Why was is `if not p.is_global` removed in the first place by the way?

dialogue.author: erikbern
dialogue.text: We can probably add back `if not p.is_global` – I was on a spree to delete as much of global parameter usage as possible. But if it causes issues let's add it back

dialogue.author: erikbern
dialogue.text: You are right that it's not fully backwards compatible. The reason I chose to go ahead and do it is that afaik `is_global` wasn't even documented so I didn't think anyone would use it

dialogue.author: Tarrasch
dialogue.text:
Well, missing test cases have been costly this time then. :)

Perhaps just change it back to the very original `if not p.is_global` rather than the almost random `if p.significant and not p.is_global`?

On the other hand, we want to eventually remove `is_global`. Without breaking this particular case of `luigi.hadoop.JobTask`. So maybe `if p.significant and not p.is_global` is the way to go after all? I'll make sure to add test cases so the next code change will be more conscious.

dialogue.author: erikbern
dialogue.text: Yeah that sounds great. Are you planning to implement it? Let me know either way

dialogue.author: Tarrasch
dialogue.text: I will fix this first thing tomorrow.

commit_message: FIX: do not let no-op monkey patches to renderer leak out
title: Matplotlib 3.2.1 savefig empty image when fig size matches data size exactly

dialogue.author: rdgraham
dialogue.text:
Matplotlib 3.2.1 savefig empty image when fig size matches data size exactly

### Bug report

**Bug summary**

Saving a figure to an image file with the same pixel dimensions as data loaded with imshow does not work in 3.2.1, producing an empty image. If the dimensions are changed by even one pixel, normal behavior returns.

This bug appeared in 3.2.1 and I have confirmed that it does not occur with 3.1.1.

I noticed in the release notes for 3.2.0 that some changes were made to the default image interpolation; perhaps an unhandled condition was introduced here when no interpolation is required. However, I note that changing the interpolation mode had no effect on this bug.  

**Code for reproduction**

```python
import numpy as np
from matplotlib import pylab as plt

x_size, y_size = (100, 100)
fig = plt.figure(frameon=False, dpi=100, figsize=(y_size/100, x_size/100))
ax = plt.Axes(fig, [0., 0., 1., 1.])
fig.add_axes(ax)
ax.set_axis_off()
ax.get_xaxis().set_visible(False)
ax.get_yaxis().set_visible(False)

data = np.random.rand(100, 100)
ax.imshow(data)
fig.savefig('/tmp/image.png', bbox_inches='tight', pad_inches=0)

# In matplotlib 3.2.1, empty file with size 100x100 pixels output
# if random data shape is changed to something other than 100x100, output
# works correctly.

# In matplotlib 3.1.1, file output is 100x100 with data shown correctly.
```

**Actual outcome**
![bug_image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3060896/83362411-df247200-a345-11ea-9175-35959d8c5cf0.png)

**Expected outcome**

![working_image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3060896/83362486-81dcf080-a346-11ea-89a8-e7c7f7b65373.png)

Produced with matplotlib 3.1.1

**Matplotlib version**
  * Operating system: Linux version 5.6.13-arch1-1
  * Matplotlib version: 3.2.1
  * Matplotlib backend (`print(matplotlib.get_backend())`): TkAgg
  * Python version: 3.8.3
  * Other libraries: numpy 1.18.4

Default system install on Arch linux package `python-matplotlib-3.2.1-1-x86_64`

dialogue.author: jklymak
dialogue.text:
If you remove `pad_inches=0` this works fine.  

This stopping working seems to bisect to a9b8b6e21d6ef1 which I think has more to do with extents than the image work.   https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/pull/16734

dialogue.author: jklymak
dialogue.text: Furthermore, if you do `pad_inches=0.0000000001` it works fine, or the axes size to 0.999999 etc it works fine.  So its definitely that you are hitting a precise integer somehow.  But if you don't do the tight_bbox, or pad_inches, you get exactly what you want.

dialogue.author: rdgraham
dialogue.text:
Thanks for the quick response and work-around. Hopefully it will be possible to get to the underlying issue.

I don't recall the origin of the pad and bbox options in this code, though I would note that it is surprising that not specifying the pad_inches and bbox_inches options results in no padding in this case. From the documentation, the default value for pad_inches is 0.1 inch.

dialogue.author: jklymak
dialogue.text: `pad_inches` only matters if you use `bbox_inches='tight'`

dialogue.author: tacaswell
dialogue.text:
This was broken by https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/pull/16734 which disables the draw methods on the renderer (because to compute the bounding boxes we don't need to actually draw turn the crank on the renderer methods, only the artist methods) but we never re-set the methods to their original values.

This is not normally a problem as the tight bounding box is a different size that original size so the caching in https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/4356b677a33430b5c9e34e8b18e07cfd630a0704/lib/matplotlib/backends/backend_agg.py#L411-L421 misses and we get a new, un-patched renderer.  However, in this case the figure is already the correct size so we hit the cache and get back the renderer with the draw methods stripped off which then results in an empty output.

I can see a bunch of hacky ways to fix this, but not sure which one is the best.

dialogue.author: jklymak
dialogue.text: It seems to me that `draw_disabled=True` should just make a new un-cached renderer?

dialogue.author: tacaswell
dialogue.text: The problem is that where we know if we have flagged it as `draw_disabled` (in backend bases) is not where we know if there is a cache or not (deep in the `print_XYZ` functions in the backends).

dialogue.author: ImportanceOfBeingErnest
dialogue.text: There is discussion in https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/16714 about whether `axhline` should use the grid's color or spline color. I suppose that would apply to `vlines/hlines` as well. So probably this needs more discussion.

dialogue.author: timhoffm
dialogue.text:
> I suppose that would apply to vlines/hlines as well.

Not necessarily. `axhline` is typically used for marking single special values (e.g. https://matplotlib.org/devdocs/gallery/pyplots/axline.html). Whereas `hlines` are more data like, so it might make sense to have them use the color cycle, but that of course, would be a breaking change, which is probably only viable for 4.0.

commit_message: Correctly set default linewidth for unfilled markers.
title: How to change the thickness of the marker "x" when using scatter?

dialogue.author: sth1997
dialogue.text:
How to change the thickness of the marker "x" when using scatter?

I'm trying to change the thickness of the marker "x" when using scatter, but I found the "linewidths" parameter doesn't work for Matplotlib version 3.1.0. 
But when I switch to Matplotlib version 1.3.1, "linewidths" parameter works well. Why? Is there something changed in newer version?
This is my code.

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import matplotlib
print(matplotlib.__version__)
x = y = np.arange(5)

fig,ax = plt.subplots(1)

ax.scatter(x,y,  s=100,marker='x',color='b',linewidths=1)
ax.scatter(x,y+1,s=100,marker='x',color='r',linewidths=2)
ax.scatter(x,y+2,s=100,marker='x',color='g',linewidths=3)

plt.show()
```
This is the figure output by Matplotlib 3.1.0.
![figure_1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16641156/79685995-6ffc2e00-826f-11ea-84b3-5199cda48238.png)

This is the figure output by Matplotlib 1.3.1.
![figure_2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16641156/79686005-99b55500-826f-11ea-9d4f-3e22d50b5901.png)

dialogue.author: ImportanceOfBeingErnest
dialogue.text:
Thanks for the report. That's indeed unexpected. A workaround for now may be to use `plot` instead of `scatter`

    ax.plot(x,y, ms=10, marker='x', mec='g', mew=3, ls="none")

or to create the marker manually and supply it to `scatter`

```
from matplotlib.markers import MarkerStyle
from matplotlib.path import Path

ms = MarkerStyle(Path([[-1,-1],[1,1], [-1,1],[1,-1]], codes=[1,2,1,2]))
ax.scatter(x,y, s=100, marker=ms, edgecolor='g', linewidths=3)
```

dialogue.author: ImportanceOfBeingErnest
dialogue.text:
The culprit is here

https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/f369b105e6153a67b4a8065ce97344b2cfbc855c/lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py#L4489-L4491

where `linewidths` is overwritten in case of unfilled markers. This makes sense for the default `linewidth=None` case, but not if something different is specified. 

When fixing this, one needs to consider that `linewidths=[None, 4, None, 3, None, 4]` is probably also a valid usecase.

dialogue.author: tmdavison
dialogue.text: A workaround for now appears to be to use `linewidth` instead of `linewidths`. I don't know why this works, but presumably that's unintended and may break in the future.

commit_message: FIX: fix support for single color 2D array as c to mean single color
title: Scatter produce multiple colors for a single RGB/RGBA input

dialogue.author: MaozGelbart
dialogue.text:
Scatter produce multiple colors for a single RGB/RGBA input

### Bug report

**Bug summary**

Scatter [docs](https://matplotlib.org/3.2.1/api/_as_gen/matplotlib.axes.Axes.scatter.html?highlight=scatter#matplotlib.axes.Axes.scatter) say that in order to provide a single RGB/RGBA color for all scatter elements, it is required to pass a 2D array with a single row for `c` argument input:

> If you want to specify the same RGB or RGBA value for all points, use a 2-D array with a single row. Otherwise, value- matching will have precedence in case of a size matching with x and y.

When x/y shape is 3 and RGB is passed as a 2D array with a single row (e.g. `c=[[1, 0.5, 0.05]]`), or when x/y shape is 4 and RGBA is passed as a 2D array with a single row, the plot elements have several different colors (taken from the default colormap?) while all should have the same color. The problem occurs for both `plt.scatter` and `ax.scatter` calls, and also if `c` input is provided as a numpy array of shape (1,3) for RGB or (1,4) for RGBA instead of a nested list. 

**Code for reproduction**

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.scatter(range(3), range(3), c=[[1, 0.5, 0.05]])
#alternatively, for RGBA scenario
plt.scatter(range(4), range(4), c=[[1, 0.5, 0.05, 1]])
```

**Actual outcome**
![840-mpl](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13831112/82084053-5d043e80-96f3-11ea-9d01-5b8afa216e0a.png)

**Expected outcome**
All points have the same color: RGB (1, 0.5, 0.05).

**Matplotlib version**
<!--Please specify your platform and versions of the relevant libraries you are using:-->
  * Operating system: Linux
  * Matplotlib version: 3.2.1
  * Matplotlib backend (`print(matplotlib.get_backend())`): agg
  * Python version: 3.7.3

<!--Please tell us how you installed matplotlib and python e.g., from source, pip, conda-->
<!--If you installed from conda, please specify which channel you used if not the default-->
Matplotlib installed through pip

dialogue.author: story645
dialogue.text:
This is a regression bug because it works as expected on 3.1.2
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1300499/82088850-835cb680-96c0-11ea-8b97-49d1056b4846.png)

dialogue.author: QuLogic
dialogue.text:
Bisects to 5a5c2f6ade2bb5cf92c9fde50ed07872e6b559f2, or #13959 by @efiring.

I'm not actually sure why we want to flatten `c` since the docs say "If you want to specify the same RGB or RGBA value for all points, use a 2-D array with a single row.", but it should work if `c` is the same length as `x`/`y`.

dialogue.author: mwaskom
dialogue.text: IMO this is a pretty serious regression; it makes seaborn plots wrong with no straightforward way for the user to work around it. Could it please get a release critical tag?

dialogue.author: tacaswell
dialogue.text:
If you know ahead of time that you want to specify an RGG(A) color not values to be colormapped you can use the `color` kwarg which has none of the ambiguity.

See #17499 for fix.

dialogue.author: mwaskom
dialogue.text: `seaborn.scatterplot` can produce varying-color scatterplots though. It would be possible to check the shape of the color array after semantic mapping and conditionally dispatch to different matplotlib kwargs, but boy would it be messy...

dialogue.author: tacaswell
dialogue.text: We currently have that code in mpl and it _is_ rather messy (which is why it keeps breaking :/ )

dialogue.author: kdpenner

LightSource.shade fails on a masked array

<!--To help us understand and resolve your issue, please fill out the form to the best of your ability.-->

### Bug report

**Bug summary**

`shade` fails on a masked array. The offender seems to be `intensity = np.clip(intensity, 0, 1)` in `shade_normals`, as `np.clip` returns a masked array with attribute `mask=False` and `shade_rgb` expects the mask attribute to be 2D.

```python
import numpy as np
from matplotlib.colors import LightSource
import matplotlib.cm as cm

delta = 0.5
x = np.arange(-3.0, 4.001, delta)
y = np.arange(-4.0, 3.001, delta)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
Z1 = np.exp(-X**2 - Y**2)
Z2 = np.exp(-(X - 1)**2 - (Y - 1)**2)
Z = Z1 - Z2

mask = np.zeros(Z.shape, dtype = bool)

Z = np.ma.masked_array(Z, mask = mask)

ls = LightSource(azdeg = 45., altdeg = 0.)

rgba = ls.shade(Z, cmap = cm.get_cmap())
```

commit_message: FIX: cancel pending autoscale on manually setting limits
title: Surprising/changed axis limit (autoscale) behavior

dialogue.author: mwaskom
dialogue.text:
Surprising/changed axis limit (autoscale) behavior

### Bug report

**Bug summary**

The interaction of setting axis limits and autoscaling changed in 3.2 with little guidance, introducing unexpected behavior that doesn't always make sense.

**Code for reproduction**

Here is a plot that looks very different on 3.1.2 and 3.2.1

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
f, ax = plt.subplots()
x = np.arange(100)
y = np.random.uniform(-.1, .1, 100)
ax.scatter(x, y)
assert ax._autoscaleYon
ax.set_ylim((-.5, .5), auto=None)
```

**Actual outcome**

On 3.2.1

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/315810/81074711-656ca600-8eb7-11ea-9929-cef357be7cf1.png)

**Expected outcome**

On 3.1.2

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/315810/81074777-79b0a300-8eb7-11ea-8510-2bddabb72dd1.png)

**Matplotlib version**
<!--Please specify your platform and versions of the relevant libraries you are using:-->
  * Operating system: macOS
  * Matplotlib version: 3.2.1
  * Matplotlib backend (`print(matplotlib.get_backend())`): pylab inline
  * Python version: 3.7

dialogue.author: mwaskom
dialogue.text: Is "autoscaling" actually documented anywhere? https://matplotlib.org/3.1.1/api/_as_gen/matplotlib.pyplot.autoscale.html just says "turn autoscaling on or off" but that's a bit tautological: it's saying "function f does f". What effect does f have?

dialogue.author: jklymak
dialogue.text:
Bisects to 160de568e1  https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/pull/13593   @anntzer  any quick idea what's going on here? 

I didn't even know about the `auto=None` option.

dialogue.author: tacaswell
dialogue.text:
https://matplotlib.org/api/_as_gen/matplotlib.axes.Axes.autoscale_view.html is the method that actually does the rescaling and has a slightly better explaination. This also gets coupled with "margins" and "sticky edges" https://matplotlib.org/gallery/subplots_axes_and_figures/axes_margins.html.

I suspect that this related to making the auto-scaling lazier.

On master I get a flash of the wrong scaling before it goes to the correct limits but it is still broken an v3.2.x.

I suspect the problem is that adding the scatter "arms" the rescale, but setting the ylim does not "disarm" it.  If you force a `f.canvas.draw()` just before setting the ylim it works correctly.

dialogue.author: jklymak
dialogue.text:
This only happens w/ `auto=None`, which says to keep the autoscaling as it was before the `set_ylim`.  So I'd argue the plot is correct since you are asking for the y-axis to be autoscaled, at least according to the docs.  

What did `auto=None` do before?  Only autoscale only if more data is added?  Thats super confusing in my opinion, but maybe seaborn has a use case?

dialogue.author: mwaskom
dialogue.text:
>  So I'd argue the plot is correct since you are asking for the y-axis to be autoscaled, at least according to the docs.

No, I'm asking for the y axis limits to be `(-.5, +.5)` at this moment in time. I am also asking for this action not to change whether or not the plot will continue to autoscale in the future.

> What did auto=None do before? Only autoscale only if more data is added?

Yes. My understanding, and the previous behavior, is that the `auto` parameter of `set_{x,y}lim` influences what happens in the future, not in response to that call.

> Thats super confusing in my opinion, but maybe seaborn has a use case?

Why is that super confusing? If I am calling a method to set explicit limits, I don't think matplotlib should ignore my limits and autoscale to narrower values.

dialogue.author: mwaskom
dialogue.text:
To be clear, there has been a change when `auto=True` as well.

```
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
f, ax = plt.subplots()
x = np.arange(20)
y = np.linspace(-.1, .1, 20)
ax.scatter(x, y)
assert ax._autoscaleYon
ax.set_ylim((-.5, .5), auto=True)
```

3.1.2:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/315810/81080934-5a1d7880-8ebf-11ea-8d70-72d8e77d5c6b.png)

3.2.1:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/315810/81081516-fe072400-8ebf-11ea-9657-0c48b0a7332e.png)

dialogue.author: anntzer
dialogue.text:
I guess
```patch
diff --git i/lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py w/lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py
index 8f60cfedd..bd727a529 100644
--- i/lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py
+++ w/lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py
@@ -3331,6 +3331,9 @@ class _AxesBase(martist.Artist):
         left, right = sorted([left, right], reverse=bool(reverse))
 
         self._viewLim.intervalx = (left, right)
+        # Mark viewlims as no longer stale without triggering an autoscale.
+        for ax in self._shared_x_axes.get_siblings(self):
+            ax._stale_viewlim_x = False
         if auto is not None:
             self._autoscaleXon = bool(auto)
 
@@ -3600,6 +3603,9 @@ class _AxesBase(martist.Artist):
         bottom, top = sorted([bottom, top], reverse=bool(reverse))
 
         self._viewLim.intervaly = (bottom, top)
+        # Mark viewlims as no longer stale without triggering an autoscale.
+        for ax in self._shared_y_axes.get_siblings(self):
+            ax._stale_viewlim_y = False
         if auto is not None:
             self._autoscaleYon = bool(auto)
```
is what we need, can you confirm that works for you?

dialogue.author: jklymak
mwaskom
dialogue.text:
> I agree, but then to autoscale if I add something new is surprising. I can see that its a useful feature, but it adds some complexity to the state of the plot that we have to keep track of, and obviously got dropped here.

Well the reason seaborn uses `auto=None` is mostly for backwards compatibility, as it matches how `ax.set{x,y}lim` had previously worked. I recall the original change causing some pain that seemed best solved at the time by just preserving the old behavior, but I don't remember the details of the issues.

dialogue.author: mwaskom
dialogue.text:
What I really want from explicit limits / autoscaling is something closer to a ratchet: I want to set limits that won't hide to-be-added data that lie outside of the limits but will also not contract when to-be-added data lie inside the limits (minus the margin).

But I'd settle for some better/less tautological documentation for how autoscale is supposed to work :)

dialogue.author: ianhi
dialogue.text:
> What I really want from explicit limits / autoscaling is something closer to a ratchet: 

@mwaskom I agree, that would be great. Until such a dream is realized I found that I could get satisfactory results like so:
```python
# for ylims only
cur_ylims = ax.get_ylim()
ax.relim()
new_lims = [ax.dataLim.y0, ax.dataLim.y0+ax.dataLim.height]
new_lims = [
        new_lims[0] if new_lims[0]<cur_ylims[0] else cur_ylims[0],
        new_lims[1] if new_lims[1]>cur_ylims[1] else cur_ylims[1]
        ]
ax.set_ylim(new_lims)
```
Full code + associated `x_lim` version lives here:
https://github.com/ianhi/mpl-interactions/blob/a5cd394514418215ed61462c5c35d33e312f52cd/mpl_interactions/jupyter.py#L64-L70

dialogue.author: mwaskom
dialogue.text:
@ianhi cleaner to write that code as

```python
ax.set_ylim(min(new_lims[0], cur_ylims[0]), max(new_lims[1], cur_ylims[1]))
```

commit_message: Lower Text's FontProperties priority when updating
title: “Size” ignored if placed before fontproperties

dialogue.author: shawnchenx6
dialogue.text:
“Size” ignored if placed before fontproperties 

<!--To help us understand and resolve your issue, please fill out the form to the best of your ability.-->
<!--You can feel free to delete the sections that do not apply.-->

### Bug report

**Bug summary**

In `as.text`, `ax.set_title` and all the functions realated to displaying characters on the figure,
the arguement `size` is ignored if it is placed before fontproperties.
<!--A short 1-2 sentences that succinctly describes the bug-->

**Code for reproduction**
```python
data = np.random.randn(10000)
plt.hist(data, bins=40, facecolor="blue", edgecolor="black", alpha=0.5)
plt.xlabel("value", fontproperties='SimHei',size=20  ) # this will work
plt.ylabel("counts",size=20, fontproperties='SimHei')  # this doesn't
plt.show()
```
This issue is replicated with ipython (%pylab) and jupyter-notebook
####

<!--A minimum code snippet required to reproduce the bug.
Please make sure to minimize the number of dependencies required, and provide
any necessary plotted data.
Avoid using threads, as Matplotlib is (explicitly) not thread-safe.-->


**Actual outcome**

<!--The output produced by the above code, which may be a screenshot, console output, etc.-->

```
# If applicable, paste the console output here
#
#
```

**Expected outcome**

<!--A description of the expected outcome from the code snippet-->
<!--If this used to work in an earlier version of Matplotlib, please note the version it used to work on-->

**Matplotlib version**
<!--Please specify your platform and versions of the relevant libraries you are using:-->
  * Operating system: win10  1909
  * Matplotlib version:  Version :3.1.2 ,   Build : py36_1 ,    conda-forge
  * Matplotlib backend (`print(matplotlib.get_backend())`):  module://ipykernel.pylab.backend_inline
  * Python version:  3.6.10
  * Jupyter version (if applicable): version  1.0.0           
  * Other libraries: 

<!--Please tell us how you installed matplotlib and python e.g., from source, pip, conda-->
<!--If you installed from conda, please specify which channel you used if not the default-->
`conda install -c conda-forge matplotlib`

dialogue.author: timhoffm
dialogue.text:
Somehow `update()` gets called with both values even though `Text()` holds `fontproperties` as explicit parameter but `size` is  handled via `**kwargs`. Since the passed arguments of `update()` are processed sequentially, order matters here.

If `fontproperties` is used with a string, a FontProperties instance with non-given defaults (including size) is created. That will overwrite the explicit `size` in the second example.

IMHO expicit font-related keywords like `size` should have precedence over `fontproperties`.

This is a good first issue to dig into. Please include a test based on the above example to validate the fix.

dialogue.author: tacaswell
dialogue.text:
However, if we get passed an actual `FontProperties` objects (not a string we implicitly up-convert) the precedence is not completely clear.    I can see an argument for in that case the order of the kwargs mattering and the last one winning, however in the case of a string input (like this example) it is pretty clear that the `size` kwarg should have higher priority.   Given that is is better to not have behavior like that depend on the exact types, `size` should always win.

Which is a very long way of saying "I agree with @timhoffm".

dialogue.author: asongtoruin
dialogue.text:
I'd be happy to take a look at this if you wouldn't mind?

From a quick dig around (and @timhoffm's comment) I think it's coming from `Artist.update()`

https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/c91d7ef888577e14a73405f8504e44d7a8113bc9/lib/matplotlib/artist.py#L968-L993

The simplest solution I can think of would be to pop `fontproperties` from the `props` dict to ensure `set_fontproperties` is run before any of the other functions. It's not particularly elegant, but it would ensure that values given by the other kwargs would always take precedence.

dialogue.author: anntzer
dialogue.text:
See https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/pull/16328 where I'm basically trying to get rid of this kind of kwargs reordering...

I think the proper solution would be for FontProperties to properly keep unset properties as being, well, unset, and not overwriting already existing properties on the text object.

Also, I guess this raises some other questions, e.g. in
```
text = ax.text(...)
text.set_fontproperties("size=24")  # or the corresponding FP object
text.set_fontproperties("SimHei")  # or the corresponding FP object
```
should the second call reset the size to the rc default?

(Note that in the base case here, passing `family="SimHei"` should work just fine, and will in fact work better if the font family contains a dash...)

dialogue.author: RiazCharania
dialogue.text: Is anyone else currently working on this? If not I'll give it a try.

dialogue.author: timhoffm
dialogue.text: @RiazCharania go for it. The first step is to clarify the expected behavior. See https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/16389#issuecomment-582181302 and https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/16389#issuecomment-585110776. I that you provide a short summary defining the behavior for all cases before implementing.

dialogue.author: HarrisonFok
dialogue.text: We'll give it a try too

commit_message: BUG: Fixed __join_multi always returning indexers (#34074) (#34075)
title: BUG: Multijoining indexes always returns indexers, ignores function parameters

dialogue.author: CuylenE
dialogue.text:
BUG: Multijoining indexes always returns indexers, ignores function parameters

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the latest version of pandas.

- [x] (optional) I have confirmed this bug exists on the master branch of pandas.

---

#### Code Sample

```python
>>>frame = pd.DataFrame([(1,2,3,4),(4,5,6,7)],columns=["i1", "i2", "i3","val"]).set_index(["i1","i2","i3"])
>>>frame2 = pd.DataFrame([(1,2,3),(4,5,6)],columns=["i1", "i2", "val"]).set_index(["i1","i2"])
>>>frame.index.join(frame2.index,return_indexers=False)
(MultiIndex([(1, 2, 3),
            (4, 5, 6)],
           names=['i1', 'i2', 'i3']), None, None)
```

#### Problem description
Parameter return_indexers is ignored in some cases of __join_multi

#### Expected Output
```python
>>>frame.index.join(frame2.index,return_indexers=False)
MultiIndex([(1, 2, 3),
            (4, 5, 6)],
           names=['i1', 'i2', 'i3'])
```

commit_message: BUG: #31464 Fix error when parsing JSON list of bool into Series (#33373)
title: read_json with typ="series" of json list of bools results in timestamps/Exception

dialogue.author: stewit
dialogue.text:
read_json with typ="series" of json list of bools results in timestamps/Exception

#### Code Sample, a copy-pastable example if possible

```python
import pandas as pd
pd.read_json('[true, true, false]', typ="series")
```
results in the following Pandas Series object in older Pandas versions:
```
0   1970-01-01 00:00:01
1   1970-01-01 00:00:01
2   1970-01-01 00:00:00
dtype: datetime64[ns]
```
Since 1.0.0 it raises TypeError: <class 'bool'> is not convertible to datetime


#### Problem description
The expected output would be a Pandas Series of bools. Note that
* with typ="frame" it works and the result is a dataframe with one column with bool values
* with convert_dates set to False correctly outputs a Series of boolean values

This is a problem because
* users would expect a Series of bools (and neither an exception nor a series of timestamps)
* it is inconsistent with the "frame" case

commit_message: BUG: func 'to_pickle' and 'read_pickle' where not accepting URL GH#30163 (#30301)
title: pandas.read_pickle can take buffer object as well, not only str

dialogue.author: anatoly-khomenko
dialogue.text:
pandas.read_pickle can take buffer object as well, not only str

https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/62a87bf4a2af02a8d3bc271ad26e5994292b8e6a/pandas/io/pickle.py#L95

read_pickle does not accept google storage URL (in form "gs://bucket-name/path/file.pkl") as input

While this code works fine:
`
with tf.io.gfile.GFile("gs://bucket-name/path/file.pkl", "rb") as infile:
      df = pd.read_pickle(infile, compression=None)
`

Might be reasonable to amend the documentation with this if the above is true for the recent version of pandas.
