commit_message: ioloop: Fix PeriodicCallback when system clock moves backwards
title: ioloop: PeriodicCallback executes too often on windows

dialogue.author: wanghongzhou
dialogue.text:
ioloop: PeriodicCallback executes too often on windows

## Here is the code:
import math

import logging
from crontab import CronTab
from tornado.ioloop import PeriodicCallback, IOLoop

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG, format='%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s')


class CronTabCallback(PeriodicCallback):

    def __init__(self, callback, schedule):
        self._callback = callback
        self._crontab = CronTab(schedule)
        super(CronTabCallback, self).__init__(self.run, self._calc_callbacktime())

    def _calc_callbacktime(self, now=None):
        return math.ceil(self._crontab.next(now)) * 1000.0

    def run(self):
        return self._callback()

    def _schedule_next(self):
        self.callback_time = self._calc_callbacktime()
        logging.info('calc ---------------------')
        logging.info('delay %s' % self.callback_time)
        logging.info('last execute %s' % self._next_timeout)

        last = self._next_timeout
        super(CronTabCallback, self)._schedule_next()

        if last == self._next_timeout:
            logging.error('error !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!')

        logging.info('current: %s' % self.io_loop.time())
        logging.info('calc next: %s' % self._next_timeout)
        logging.info('--------------------------\n')


def crontab(schedule):
    def decorator(func):
        CronTabCallback(func, schedule).start()
        return func

    return decorator


@crontab('*/1 * * * *')
def run():
    logging.info('execute ... \n')


if __name__ == '__main__':
    IOLoop.current().start()

## Here is the console log
2018-03-30 11:33:00,311 - asyncio - DEBUG - Using selector: SelectSelector
2018-03-30 11:33:00,316 - root - INFO - calc ---------------------
2018-03-30 11:33:00,316 - root - INFO - delay 60000.0
2018-03-30 11:33:00,316 - root - INFO - last execute 1522380780.3169544
2018-03-30 11:33:00,316 - root - INFO - current: 1522380780.3169544
2018-03-30 11:33:00,316 - root - INFO - **calc next: 1522380840.3169544**
2018-03-30 11:33:00,316 - root - INFO - --------------------------

**2018-03-30 11:34:00,313** - root - INFO - execute ... 

2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - INFO - calc ---------------------
2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - INFO - delay 60000.0
2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - INFO - last execute 1522380840.3169544
2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - ERROR - error !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - INFO - current: 1522380840.3139544
2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - INFO - calc next: 1522380840.3169544
2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - INFO - --------------------------

2018-03-30 11:34:00,318 - root - INFO - execute ... 

2018-03-30 11:34:00,318 - root - INFO - calc ---------------------
2018-03-30 11:34:00,318 - root - INFO - delay 60000.0
2018-03-30 11:34:00,318 - root - INFO - last execute 1522380840.3169544
2018-03-30 11:34:00,318 - root - INFO - current: 1522380840.3189545
2018-03-30 11:34:00,318 - root - INFO - calc next: 1522380900.3169544
2018-03-30 11:34:00,318 - root - INFO - --------------------------

## Environment:
OS: windows 7
Python:  python 3.6
Dependent library: crontab 0.22.0

Tornado 4.5.1 python2 (ok) python3(ok)
Tornado 5.0.1 python2 (ok) python3 (linux ok, window has issue)

dialogue.author: wanghongzhou
dialogue.text:
## The following code will be executed twice every minute

import logging
from tornado.ioloop import PeriodicCallback, IOLoop

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG, format='%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s')

def run():
    logging.info('execute ... \n')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    PeriodicCallback(run, 60000).start()
    IOLoop.current().start()

dialogue.author: bdarnell
dialogue.text: Standard disclaimer: Windows is not a fully-supported platform for Tornado. I think this is because `time.time()` on windows is very low resolution, and there is a bug in the logic in `PeriodicCallback._schedule_next` if we can complete our callback faster than the clock changes. I'm not sure why it doesn't happen on python 2, though.

dialogue.author: wanghongzhou
dialogue.text: The same python3 environment 4.5 why there is no problem, there is a problem with 5.0.1, is this the problem of the operating system?

dialogue.author: bdarnell
dialogue.text:
I'm not sure. The big change in 5.0 is that on python 3 we always use the asyncio event loop. So the asyncio loop is acting differently from the old tornado loop somehow. 

Maybe it's because asyncio uses a different (monotonic) clock internally but tornado is using `time.time()` which is subject to ntp adjustments. Those shouldn't be that frequent, though. 

In any case, it definitely looks like PeriodicCallback has a bug when the callback completes faster than the resolution of the clock, so it should be fixed there. It's not windows' fault, but the fact that it's only a problem on windows is why it hasn't been noticed before.

dialogue.author: wanghongzhou
dialogue.text:
I think the solution to this problem is to find the root of the problem first, and then solve it from the root, rather than avoid it by modifying the logic of PeriodicCallback._schedule_next.
I don't know why it's specific, so I can't solve it, but I will continue to pay attention to it. Because my English is not good, these are translated by means of translation tools. I hope it doesn't bring trouble to you. Thank you for reading.

dialogue.author: bdarnell
dialogue.text:
OK, so looking at these timestamps, the first time through the loop (For clarity, I will only use the three digits left and right of the decimal point):

```
2018-03-30 11:33:00,316 - root - INFO - current: 1522380780.3169544
2018-03-30 11:33:00,316 - root - INFO - calc next: 1522380840.3169544
```

It is now 780.316, so the next run should be 60s later, at 840.316. But the next run wakes up early, at 840.313:

    2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - INFO - current: 1522380840.3139544

Since this is less than the "next" time, we use the same "next" time for the following run.

In Tornado's IOLoop, we perform an additional check of the current time before scheduling a callback:

https://github.com/tornadoweb/tornado/blob/35a538f50e704e348926e1b113bc03328a1da9f2/tornado/ioloop.py#L985-L997

If your system clock moves backwards, this will effectively delay the callback, which hides the time adjustment from higher levels unless it occurs between the IOLoop scheduling the callback and it running.

Asyncio does a [similar check of the current time](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/5a7092de1226a95a50f0f384eea8ddb288959249/Lib/asyncio/base_events.py#L1679), but *it uses a different clock*. Specifically, Tornado uses `time.time()`, which is subject to NTP adjustments, while asyncio uses `time.monotonic`, which is not (although it may be slewed in an effort to minimize discrepancies between the two clocks; this happens on linux according to PEP 418).

So I think I was wrong to blame the low precision of windows clocks. This can happen on any system where `time.time()` runs slower than `time.monotonic()`. Linux can avoid minor differences here by slewing the monotonic clock, but when larger clock adjustments are required it can happen there too. 

We need to either remove this `if` or give it an `else`. We always want to move the time forward. I think just removing the `if` will do the right thing.

https://github.com/tornadoweb/tornado/blob/35a538f50e704e348926e1b113bc03328a1da9f2/tornado/ioloop.py#L1218-L1221

dialogue.author: bdarnell
dialogue.text: I think I have a fix for this in #2338. Can you try it out?

dialogue.author: wanghongzhou
dialogue.text:
I tested it and it will not be executed twice now. Because each time will be a little bit earlier, after a longer period, the number of executions will be less than expected.

Self._next_timeout = current_time + callback_time_sec

Should this code be written like this?

Self._next_timeout += callback_time_sec

dialogue.author: bdarnell
dialogue.text:
The problem is that there are two different clocks (time.time() and time.monotonic()) running at two different speeds. Which one should we use? Tornado used to use time.time() for everything; asyncio has always used time.monotonic(). 

What do these do on your machine?

 ```
import time
while True:
    print(time.time())
    time.sleep(60)
```

```
import asyncio, time

async def f():
    while True:
        print(time.time())
        await asyncio.sleep(60)
asyncio.get_event_loop.run_until_complete(f())
```

I think that you'll see these are also running slightly faster than once per minute according to time.time(). (but they're running on schedule according to time.monotonic())

If the clock is moving backwards just a little bit because of the difference between time.time() and time.monotonic(), then `self._next_timeout += callback_time_sec`. But if the clock moves backwards for any other reason, or if it moves backwards by a lot, it's better to reset to `current_time + callback_time_sec`. I think since it's impossible to tell why the clock is moving backwards (or which clock is more accurate if they're different), it's better to use `current_time + callback_time_sec`.

dialogue.author: bdarnell
dialogue.text:
> I think since it's impossible to tell why the clock is moving backwards (or which clock is more accurate if they're different), it's better to use current_time + callback_time_sec.

I've changed my mind about this. When there's a difference between the two, it's generally going to be because time.time() is getting synchronized via NTP with an external source, so it will be more accurate. I'll change this to your suggestion of `self._next_timeout += callback_time_sec`. 

If there's a large jump backwards, then we may have to wait a while before the next run of the callback. But A) large backwards jumps shouldn't really happen and B) depending on when the jump occurs, there's not much we can do about it anyway (we can only reliably detect backwards jumps that occur during the execution of the callback. In between callbacks we have to rely on what the event loop itself does)

commit_message: asyncio: Fix a leak when event loops are created and destroyed
title: Synchronous HTTPClient leaks memory in 5.0.1

dialogue.author: NicolasLM
dialogue.text:
Synchronous HTTPClient leaks memory in 5.0.1

The synchronous HTTPClient leaks memory for each request made since upgrading to Tornado 5.0.1.

```python
from tornado import httpclient

while True:
    try:
        http_client = httpclient.HTTPClient()
        response = http_client.fetch("http://localhost")
    except Exception:
        pass
    finally:
        http_client.close()
```

Running this script for 3 minutes in docker (`python:3.6-stretch`): 
- `tornado==4.5.3`: 18.52MiB / 1.952GiB
- `tornado==5.0.1`: 350.28MiB / 1.952GiB

Note, I cannot seem to reproduce the issue on macOS.

dialogue.author: DreamHackchosenone
dialogue.text:
about httpclient.HTTPClient()
I can't execute **http_client = httpclient.HTTPClient()**  in tornado==5.0
running this will throw Exception this：
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/zhonghao/anaconda3/envs/tornado_test/lib/python3.6/site-packages/tornado/httpclient.py", line 85, in __del__
    self.close()
  File "/Users/zhonghao/anaconda3/envs/tornado_test/lib/python3.6/site-packages/tornado/httpclient.py", line 89, in close
    if not self._closed:
AttributeError: 'HTTPClient' object has no attribute '_closed'

but in tornado==4.5.2
it works fine, this issue on macOS，can you tell me why？

dialogue.author: bdarnell
dialogue.text:
Thanks for the report! I can easily reproduce this on macOS (with python 3.6. the issue doesn't occur with python 2. Which version were you using in your macos test?). The leak occurs when an IOLoop is created and destroyed, so if you create a single HTTPClient and reuse it, the leak goes away.

The cause of the leak is [`IOLoop._ioloop_for_asyncio`](https://github.com/tornadoweb/tornado/blob/fb74e4816ccfa7fc6a7abd8c8aab1f415cfc1b13/tornado/ioloop.py#L188), which is a `WeakKeyDictionary`. However, there is a strong reference from the IOLoop to the asyncio loop, which prevents the asyncio loop from ever being garbage collected. 

A fix for this particular issue is easy: when the IOLoop is closed, we can break up the reference cycle. But this still effectively turns the WeakKeyDictionary into a strongly-referenced dict. Is there a way to rearrange the reference graph so that asyncio event loops remain garbage collectable in the normal way?

commit_message: Fix HTTP1Connection for responses without content-length
title: HTTP1Connection cannot handle http/1.0 304 response

dialogue.author: beanyoung
dialogue.text:
HTTP1Connection cannot handle http/1.0 304 response

When HTTP1Connection receive a http/1.0 304 response, `start_line` at 
[this line](https://github.com/tornadoweb/tornado/blob/7a96bcf125740af78995b49526cbad688bfba796/tornado/http1connection.py#L484) is `ResponseStartLine(version='HTTP/1.0', code=304, reason='Not Modified')`, and `_can_keep_alive` crash.

dialogue.author: bdarnell
dialogue.text: That's definitely a bug, but I'm curious how you ran into this because the only client-side use of `HTTP1Connection` in Tornado itself sets `no_keep_alive` to True and avoids the bug. Are you using HTTP1Connection directly? That's great, but this is the first I've heard of anyone doing that and the interface may not be entirely stable yet (as we introduce a parallel HTTP2 connection class).

dialogue.author: beanyoung
dialogue.text: I'm writing a [http proxy](https://github.com/BeanYoung/green-proxy) using tornado. I use `HTTP1Connection` directly in the http proxy.

commit_message: support `bool()` cast when `disable=True`
title: Can't evaluate tqdm as a boolean if disable is True

dialogue.author: CharlesB2
dialogue.text:
Can't evaluate tqdm as a boolean if disable is True

If I convert a tqdm object to boolean to test for existence, I get an AttributeError if it was created with `disable=True`. Instead I would expect it to evaluate to False.

```
In [3]: pbar = tqdm(disable=False, total=100)
  0%|                                                                              | 0/100 [00:00<?, ?it/s]
In [4]: bool(pbar)
Out[4]: True

In [5]: pbar = tqdm(disable=True, total=100)

In [6]: bool(pbar)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-6-da3b9c36e33c> in <module>()
----> 1 bool(pbar)

~/prog/vopu/lib/python3.5/site-packages/tqdm/_tqdm.py in __len__(self)
    867 
    868     def __len__(self):
--> 869         return self.total if self.iterable is None else \
    870             (self.iterable.shape[0] if hasattr(self.iterable, "shape")
    871              else len(self.iterable) if hasattr(self.iterable, "__len__")

AttributeError: 'tqdm' object has no attribute 'total'
In [7]: print(tqdm.__version__, sys.version, sys.platform)
4.23.4 3.5.2 (default, Nov 23 2017, 16:37:01) 
[GCC 5.4.0 20160609] linux
```

- [x] I have visited the [source website], and in particular
  read the [known issues]
- [x] I have searched through the [issue tracker] for duplicates
- [x] I have mentioned version numbers, operating system and
  environment, where applicable:
  ```python
  import tqdm, sys
  print(tqdm.__version__, sys.version, sys.platform)
  ```

  [source website]: https://github.com/tqdm/tqdm/
  [known issues]: https://github.com/tqdm/tqdm/#faq-and-known-issues
  [issue tracker]: https://github.com/tqdm/tqdm/issues?q=

dialogue.author: casperdcl
dialogue.text:
I wonder if `False` should be the expected behaviour, though. Consider:

```python
from tqdm import tqdm

a = range(9)
bool(a)  # True

b = []
bool(b)  # False

ta = tqdm(a)
bool(ta)  # True

tb = tqdm(b)
bool(tb)  # False

tad = tqdm(a, disable=True)
bool(tad)  # True

ttd = tqdm(total=9, disable=True)
bool(ttd)  # shouldn't this also be True?
```

dialogue.author: CharlesB2
dialogue.text: Indeed, last one should be True. But then maybe a bit of documentation should be added because one could expect a visabled tqdm object to be False :-)

dialogue.author: chengs
dialogue.text:
IMHO, this last two cases, ttb should be always False. As @CharlesB2 said, I am the one who will expect disabled tqdm to be False...
```
tad = tqdm(a, disable=True)
bool(tad)  # True

ttd = tqdm(total=9, disable=True)
bool(ttd)  # shouldn't this also be True?
```

dialogue.author: casperdcl
dialogue.text: But `tqdm` is made to be a transparent wrapper around an iterable, and thus in *all* cases, `func(a)` should have exactly the same behaviour as `func(tqdm(a))`, excluding the side-effect of having a progress bar.

dialogue.author: chengs
dialogue.text: @casperdcl if this is your design choice, then I think the commit is fine.

commit_message: fix `disable=True` edge case
title: 'tqdm' object has no attribute 'total' when using 'disable' and multiprocessing 'Pool'

dialogue.author: NOhs
dialogue.text:
'tqdm' object has no attribute 'total' when using 'disable' and multiprocessing 'Pool'

The following MWE shows how to get the error message described in the title:

```python
from tqdm import tqdm
from multiprocessing import Pool

def f(arg):
    pass

if __name__ == '__main__':
    pool = Pool()
    list(tqdm(pool.imap(f, range(100)), disable=True))
```

- tqdm Version: 4.22.0
- python 3.6.0
- Win64

It works, when the `disable=True` is removed or set to `False`. But it should also work for the disabled case I would guess.

dialogue.author: casperdcl
dialogue.text: confirmed on linux py35

$ seq 1000 | tqdm --total 1000 --desc 'test-test' > /dev/null

Error:
Usage:
  tqdm [--help | options]
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/tqdm/_main.py", line 144, in main
    tqdm_args[o] = cast(v, opt_types[o])
KeyError: 'test'

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

    load_entry_point('tqdm==4.19.5', 'console_scripts', 'tqdm')()
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/tqdm/_main.py", line 146, in main

dialogue.author: casperdcl
dialogue.text: hah, nice bug - my hacky dependency-free parsing to blame.

dialogue.author: freeboson
dialogue.text: @casperdcl why not use `argparse`?

dialogue.author: casperdcl
dialogue.text: There's something nice about being completely dependency-free (it's not built-in for python 2.6, and would require us to basically implement `argopt` (or `docopt`) for us to support docstring-derived CLI parameters. Anyway I just realised my `devel` branch commit perfectly solves this issue (`tqdm --desc 'foo-bar'` is fine, and `tqdm --desc '-foobar'` isn't, which is normal for CLI programs)

dialogue.author: yan12125

Invalid timestamps in TTML are ignored now. In the next version you can embed subtitles with the following command:

```
$ youtube-dl -v -f hls-2364 --write-sub --sub-format ttml --convert-subtitles srt --embed-subs https://tv.nrk.no/serie/tara-og-vegard/MYNT16000115/sesong-1/episode-1

dialogue.author: VolatileCable

dialogue.author: someziggyman
dialogue.text: Chapters are still NULL in 2020.07.28 release

dialogue.author: Autissima
dialogue.text: Doesn't work on current master branch, #26297 fixes this.

dialogue.author: sheerluck
dialogue.text:
> #26297 fixes this.

#26297 fixes this for https://youtu.be/7LFJ7zSFUa8 and https://youtu.be/fzK79PgKITI
I wonder maybe youtube-dl could split video into chapters? (sudden feature request: to add `--split-into-chapters` so several files would be generated)

dialogue.author: Autissima
dialogue.text:
I think that request was already made a couple of times, don't remember what was said about it though.
There are a lot of splits that do exactly that though:
https://github.com/wattux/youtube-dl/commit/dcab7389acae63f0d2e01e13257e840e00c40518

If something breaks, you can just copy the new part of the postprocessor, and put it into a new version of ytdl.
You might need to do some tweaking though, since some youtube videos have filenames that windows normally wouldn't allow.

dialogue.author: sheerluck
dialogue.text:
Thank you very very much!
Works like a charm!
https://i.imgur.com/DAiXKg6.png
My filenames tweaking is
```patch
--- a/youtube_dl/utils.py
+++ b/youtube_dl/utils.py
@@ -2091,6 +2091,12 @@ def sanitize_filename(s, restricted=False, is_id=False):
             return '' if restricted else '\''
         elif char == ':':
             return '_-' if restricted else ' -'
+        elif char == '|':
+            return chr(65372)
+        elif char == '/':
+            return chr(10744)
+        elif char == '\\':
+            return chr(10745)
         elif char in '\\/|*<>':
             return '_'
         if restricted and (char in '!&\'()[]{}$;`^,#' or char.isspace()):
```

dialogue.author: Autissima
dialogue.text:
That's a way more elegant solution than mine, I just changed the def _build_track_name to replace characters with a space.

But glad to have helped, still would be real nice to see this functionality implemented into YTDL one day :^)

Only problem that I had so far is that it apparently creates a second track per file with length 0, and if you open a split file in f.e. F2K, it shows two tracks, but one is immediately skipped over, nothing unusable, just annoying.

dialogue.author: cantalupo555
dialogue.text:
Use a current fork, like yt-dlp.
It supports Chapters.

Make sure you are using the latest version; see  https://yt-dl.org/update  on how to update. Be sure to call youtube-dl with the --verbose flag and include its complete output.
```

### Description of your *issue*, suggested solution and other information

Most twitch clips work just fine, but this specific one causes problems (https://clips.twitch.tv/CarelessZealousKangarooNerfBlueBlaster).

Looking at the js/json string being parsed suggests that its `segments` value, which is `null` for all working clips including youtube-dl's test URLs, is the culprit. The _original_ form of this line is

```segments: [{"playback_offset":0,"offset":-3.885780586188048e-16,"duration":39.75000000000001,"speed":1}],```

which gets transformed into

```"segments": [{"playback_offset":0,"offset":-3.885780586188048"e"-16,"duration":39.75000000000001,"speed":1}],```

This adds quotation marks around the exponent marker which causes the error.

$ youtube-dl -f worst "https://www.itv.com/hub/royal-stories/2a4353a0004" -o "The People's Princess[2a4353a0004].mp4" --write-sub --convert-subs=srt

dialogue.author: Vangelis66
dialogue.text:
@yan12125 wrote: 

> Sorry I didn't check carefully. It should really be fixed in the next version

Hi :smile: I hope you're well ! 
No need to apologise; as ever, your constant efforts and user support is greatly appreciated (this goes to the whole team BTW :+1: )

So, after a wait of several days, next (latest) version **2017.09.24** was released and I was eager to try it out: 
```
youtube-dl -f worst "https://www.itv.com/hub/the-jonathan-ross-show/2a1166a0135" --write-sub --convert-subs=srt -k
```
As expected, now the subs conversion from .ttml to .srt _completes successfully_; for the record, out of the major software players on the Windows platform, only VLC 3.0.0-git appears to have native support for the .ttml (dfxp) subs format; but I usually use MPC-BE / MPC-HC / PotPlayer, which all require the conversion to .srt, hence the original issue that was filed...

Trying the generated .srt file with MPC-BE, I almost immediately saw there must be a **flaw somewhere in the conversion from .ttml -> .srt**, which is demonstrated as the following issue: 
1. When a sub contains two (or more) lines and the **font colour is white**, everything looks OK: 

![white_2lines](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9669492/30783948-8d0c7f58-a154-11e7-9bfe-753bc25cdd39.jpg)

2. However, in every **two-line subtitle** where **font colour !=white** (even with different colours between lines), **the font size in the second line is noticeably bigger that the first one**: 

![green_2lines](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9669492/30783979-2f2b6402-a155-11e7-805f-4f4ede4c29d7.JPG)
![yellow_2lines](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9669492/30783981-36a9bab2-a155-11e7-9337-f843aa438626.jpg)
![blue_2lines](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9669492/30783984-3c01bd0c-a155-11e7-94bf-8ed74ecfafb9.jpg)
![2colours_2lines](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9669492/30783986-3eb52700-a155-11e7-82e7-6e65ab1c5343.jpg)

Unfortunately, I know nothing about Python, this is not something I can fix myself and submit a PR, so again it should be investigated and hopefully remedied by a dev...

A personal comment/suggestion: 
I see that the .ttml subs is the first item to be downloaded by youtube-dl, folowed by the video itself, 
the subs conversion is the last step; maybe consider putting the subs conversion right after fetching the .ttml file (this would avoid waiting for the video download to complete before grabbing the .srt file)? 
In a related matter, ITV Hub usually first publish a fresh video without the accompanying subs, these may (or in rare cases may not) be added at a later stage; AFAICS, there's not a **--subs-only** switch in youtube-dl so as to fetch only the subs once they become available; reading the docs it would appear 
` --skip-download --write-sub --convert-subs=srt -k` 
should be able to achieve the same thing, but, sadly, I am left with only the .ttml subs (because subs conversion follows after video fetch... :disappointed: ). 
For your convenience and further testing, I am attaching here both .ttml+.srt subs produced by latest youtube-dl: [ITV_Subs_bug.zip](https://github.com/rg3/youtube-dl/files/1327445/ITV_Subs_bug.zip)
And I'm not sure if it's at all helpful, but here's a [link](https://github.com/monsieurvideo/get-flash-videos/blob/master/lib/FlashVideo/Utils.pm#L294-L374) how get-flash-videos handles the subs conversion in Perl...
A million thanks for this excellent tool, along with your consideration for disabled people (my sister is hard of hearing, so relies on .srt subs...).

dialogue.author: yan12125
dialogue.text:
> I am left with only the .ttml subs (because subs conversion follows after video fetch... 😞 ).

Yep that's another bug (caused by me!). Please follow #9073 for updates.

For the issue of larger texts, could you try the SRT on more players? At the first glance, the transformed srt for this video seems correct.

dialogue.author: dstftw
dialogue.text:
Neither VLC nor mpv have such problem. Most likely MPC does not support nested tags properly.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1908898/30784680-21e0f4b8-a184-11e7-9ec6-4e49fc48bfde.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1908898/30784688-66729618-a184-11e7-8c19-27031dde5441.png)

dialogue.author: Vangelis66
dialogue.text:
... Thank you both for your input/suggestions. 

yan12125 wrote: 

> For the issue of larger texts, could you try the SRT on more players? 

Well, being on a Sunday I had some more free time to check, and: 
**MPC-HC** (stable release and latest beta) exhibits the same behaviour as described in https://github.com/rg3/youtube-dl/issues/14191#issuecomment-331720612; in default video dimensions/maximised window/full screen, second line of coloured subtitle has larger fonts. 
**MPC-BE** (my preferred video player), being a fork of MPC-HC, as expected has the same "issue"; let me just say for the record that I've been using MPC-HC for almost a decade (WinXP/Vista/7), for the last year or so switched to MPC-BE (on Vista/7), and it's the first time a flavour of .srt subtitles isn't rendered properly... 😕 
**PotPlayer** is a mixed bag case; the .srt file produced by yt-dl has the "issue" I reported when viewing the video in default dimensions, but the "issue" goes away in maximised window/full screen modes; even more bizarrely, this "issue" goes completely away, in all possible window modes, if I make the subtitle fonts **bold** (???). So, using PotPlayer with bold fonts is one solution I could change to...

dstftw wrote: 

>Neither VLC nor mpv have such problem.

I do have a portable installation of VLC 2.2.6 on this Vista laptop and can confirm; however, I mostly use VLC as an audio stream recorder rather than a video viewer; be that as it may, **when I full-screen VLC with this certain type of .srt file produced by youtube-dl, the subtitles remain extremely small**: 

![fs](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9669492/30787509-e866302c-a192-11e7-98c4-054b5004c6e5.jpg)

Again, to overcome this flaw in VLC and read subs in full-screen I have to go into its advanced options and unselect subtitle formatting (resulting in monochrome subs 👎) ... 
 To conclude, _I do insist the ITV .srt subs produced by yt-dl have a (small) flaw_; you, OTOH, state: 

> At first glance, the transformed srt for this video seems correct. 

and 

> Most likely MPC does not support nested tags properly. 

(blaming in essence the player...). 
I'm not a coder so can't challenge your claims, in fact I'm overly grateful for this app; your coding time is probably better spent fixing other crucial yt-dl issues than this small subtitles handicap... If others are also OK with current status, I personally will further process those .srt subs with SubtitleEdit (or similar) to strip formatting and create a look of them I'm more comfortable with... 

Again, many thanks!

title: Error when using `external_downloader` = curl without explicitly specifying `continuedl`

dialogue.author: char101
dialogue.text:
Error when using `external_downloader` = curl without explicitly specifying `continuedl`

- [x] I've **verified** and **I assure** that I'm running youtube-dl **2017.08.06**
- [x] At least skimmed through the [README](https://github.com/rg3/youtube-dl/blob/master/README.md), **most notably** the [FAQ](https://github.com/rg3/youtube-dl#faq) and [BUGS](https://github.com/rg3/youtube-dl#bugs) sections
- [x] [Searched](https://github.com/rg3/youtube-dl/search?type=Issues) the bugtracker for similar issues including closed ones

### What is the purpose of your *issue*?
- [x] Bug report (encountered problems with youtube-dl)
- [ ] Site support request (request for adding support for a new site)
- [ ] Feature request (request for a new functionality)
- [ ] Question
- [ ] Other

---

In `CurlFD`, this line

```python
cmd += self._bool_option('--continue-at', 'continuedl', '-', '0')
```

will fail since `continuedl` value is `None`, unless it is explicitly specified in the options.

Bug when '$PATH' string contained in Video Title

## Please follow the guide below

- You will be asked some questions and requested to provide some information, please read them **carefully** and answer honestly
- Put an `x` into all the boxes [ ] relevant to your *issue* (like that [x])
- Use *Preview* tab to see how your issue will actually look like

---

### Make sure you are using the *latest* version: run `youtube-dl --version` and ensure your version is *2017.07.09*. If it's not read [this FAQ entry](https://github.com/rg3/youtube-dl/blob/master/README.md#how-do-i-update-youtube-dl) and update. Issues with outdated version will be rejected.
- [x] I've **verified** and **I assure** that I'm running youtube-dl **2017.07.09**

### Before submitting an *issue* make sure you have:

- [x] [Searched](https://github.com/rg3/youtube-dl/search?type=Issues) the bugtracker for similar issues including closed ones

### What is the purpose of your *issue*?
- [x] Bug report (encountered problems with youtube-dl)
- [ ] Site support request (request for adding support for a new site)
- [ ] Feature request (request for a new functionality)
- [ ] Question
- [ ] Other

---

### The following sections concretize particular purposed issues, you can erase any section (the contents between triple ---) not applicable to your *issue*

---

### If the purpose of this *issue* is a *bug report*, *site support request* or you are not completely sure provide the full verbose output as follows:

Add `-v` flag to **your command line** you run youtube-dl with, copy the **whole** output and insert it here. It should look similar to one below (replace it with **your** log inserted between triple ```):
```
$ ./youtube-dl -v "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rJMFxIbDe-g"                           
[debug] System config: []                                                         
[debug] User config: []
[debug] Custom config: []
[debug] Command-line args: ['-v', 'https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rJMFxIbDe-g']
[debug] Encodings: locale UTF-8, fs utf-8, out UTF-8, pref UTF-8
[debug] youtube-dl version 2017.07.09
[debug] Python version 3.6.1 - Linux-4.12.0-gentoo-x86_64-Intel-R-_Core-TM-_i5-3210M_CPU_@_2.50GHz-with-gentoo-2.4.1
[debug] exe versions: ffmpeg 3.3.2, ffprobe 3.3.2
[debug] Proxy map: {}
[youtube] rJMFxIbDe-g: Downloading webpage
[youtube] rJMFxIbDe-g: Downloading video info webpage
[youtube] rJMFxIbDe-g: Extracting video information
[youtube] rJMFxIbDe-g: Downloading MPD manifest
[debug] Invoking downloader on
[download] Destination: Everything You Need to Know About /usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu/gcc-bin/6.3.0:/usr/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu/i686-elf/gcc-bin/5.4.0:/usr/lib/llvm/4/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/opt/bin:/usr/games/bin in Bash-rJMFxIbDe-g.f137.mp4
[download]   4.9% of 14.43MiB at 71.53KiB/s ETA 03:16^C
ERROR: Interrupted by user

...
<end of log>
```

---

### Description of your *issue*, suggested solution and other information

This issue occured when I was downloading a tutorial video about Bash that contained $PATH in the title. The video URL is https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rJMFxIbDe-g. When youtube-dl attempts to create the file with the video title to download into the $PATH string in the title actually gets replaced with the contents of the actual $PATH variable in my shell. As you can see by the line in the output showing the destination file this creates a huge mess of directories with the mistitled video sitting at the bottom of it. Videos containing $ are rare but it needs to be escaped, I'm happy to provide any additional information.

**Please Note:** While I respect the request for the whole output I removed the URL on the line "[debug] Invoking downloader on" as I know it is not relevant to the issue whatsoever but it contained informtion such as my IP address.

```

I know I can specify the channel in the video URL, but I want to use my subscription list and pick the video format depending on the channel.  I don't want to unsubscribe from these channels.  I need to exclude those channels with the last catch-all download line in the script using match-format "uploader != xxxx & uploader != xxxx & uploader != xxxx "

Here is that part of my script:

```
youtube-dl -f 299+141/299+140 https://www.youtube.com/user/blkdog7/videos -o "%%(uploader)s/%%(title)s.%%(ext)s" --ignore-errors --download-archive archive.YT --verbose --restrict-filenames 
youtube-dl -f 299+141/299+140 https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC1hlBVlxLDY--Ih2fEyH5nQ/videos  -o "%%(uploader)s/%%(title)s.%%(ext)s" --ignore-errors --download-archive archive.YT --verbose --restrict-filenames 
youtube-dl -f 299+141/299+140/137+141/137+140/bestvideo+bestaudio/best -o "%%(uploader)s/%%(title)s.%%(ext)s" --ignore-errors --download-archive archive.YT -u username -p password :ytsubs --verbose --restrict-filenames  --match-filter "uploader_id != blkdog7 & uploader_id != UC1hlBVlxLDY--Ih2fEyH5nQ"

```

dialogue.author: gohlhausen
dialogue.text:
It looks like a simple change to the regex will add this functionality.
I don't know what the impact is to the rest of the project, though.

Change line 1930 in utils.py from

```
            (?P<strval>(?![0-9.])[a-z0-9A-Z]*)
```

to

```
            (?P<strval>(?![0-9.])[a-z0-9A-Z/s/-]*)
```

This will allow whitespaces and dashes to be considered part of a strval.
Since the input is already broken by & before it gets here, there shouldn't be any problem with the function knowing what part is what.

dialogue.author: mariussteffen
dialogue.text:
I've had a similar problem, but instead of '-', '_' was causing an exception.
So the regex should be:
`(?P<strval>(?![0-9.])[a-z0-9A-Z/s/-]*)`

EDIT: Sometimes ids start with a number. Currently, when excluding such ids with `id != <id>`, **NOTHING** will pass the filter.
So to decide whether a value should be parsed as integer or string, strings should always start and end with `'`. So maybe something like:
`(?P<strval>\'[a-z0-9A-Z/s/-]*\')`

dialogue.author: Snafu
dialogue.text: Sorry to dig this one up again. I'm trying to convert a TTML file to a SRT file because neither MPC-HC nor VLC will play the TTML files. Is there a way to just use the subtitle converter without downloading anything to YouTube but use a local file? Like yan12125 used the demo file posted here. From what I read in the documentation it only works with YT URIs.

dialogue.author: yan12125
dialogue.text:
Currently no simple way. You can write a Python wrapper:
```Python
from youtube_dl.utils import dfxp2srt
with open('xxx.ttml') as f:
    srt_data = dfxp2srt(f.read())
with open('yyy.srt') as f:
    f.write(srt_data)
```

dialogue.text:

The goal here is either "everything in this directory", or the command, "convert_srt *.ttml".

dialogue.author: yan12125
dialogue.text: @keybounce: you can use ```postprocessors``` key of ```YoutubeDL```. Check ```__init__.py``` for examples.

dialogue.author: jaimeMF
dialogue.text:
The problem was that the Facebook extractor didn't recognize the url. It will work in the next release, thanks for the report.

In the last output you posted it worked because you have removed the `fref=ts#!` part, the `The syntax of the command is incorrect.` message is because you haven't escaped the `&` or quoted the url

commit_message: Fix #2355 (date parsing with dashes)
title: ArchiveOrg test fail upload_date

dialogue.author: ghost
dialogue.text:
ArchiveOrg test fail upload_date

Upload date is in format `1968-12-10`, but in `unified_strdate` in utils.py re.sub don't work.

```
re.sub(r' ?(\+|-)[0-9:]*$', '', date_str)
```

It cuts down day like this `1968-12`. Unfortunatelly i'm not good with regex to fix this :(

dialogue.author: phihag
dialogue.text: Thanks, fixed.
